Smart Panels Compared: SPAN, Leviton, Lumin and More
An educational tour of the real smart panel options — SPAN, Leviton, Lumin, Savant, Eaton — and how their different designs fit different homes and budgets.

There is no single winner in smart panels, because the products on the market aren't variations of one design — they're genuinely different architectures aimed at different homes. Some replace your whole panel; some snap smart breakers into a load center; some bolt a smart subpanel beside what you already have. This guide walks through the real options — SPAN, Leviton, Lumin, Savant, and Eaton's smart breakers — and explains *why* each design fits some situations better than others, so you can match the architecture to your home rather than shopping by brand buzz. We haven't lab-tested these units, so you won't find rankings here — just the structural differences that actually drive the decision. ## Three architectures, not one product
The market splits into three design philosophies
🏠Full replacementSwap the whole panel for one with intelligence built into every circuit (SPAN).
🔧Smart breakers in a load centerA conventional-style panel that accepts smart breakers where you want them (Leviton; Eaton AbleEdge in compatible panels).
➕Add-on beside your panelKeep your existing panel; add a smart subpanel or modules for chosen circuits (Lumin, Savant).
Which architecture fits you is mostly decided by two facts about your house: whether your current panel is worth keeping, and how many circuits you truly want smart. A failing or full panel points to replacement; a healthy panel with focused needs points to the add-on or breaker routes. That single framing sorts most of the market. ## SPAN: the all-in replacement SPAN's panel is the most software-forward take: replace the box entirely and every circuit becomes individually monitored and controllable from one app. It was the first panel certified to UL 3141, the power-control-systems standard, and it targets exactly the whole-home cases this site covers — [fitting EV charging on tight service](/smart-panel-vs-main-panel-upgrade/), prioritizing circuits during [battery backup](/smart-panel-vs-transfer-switch/), and coordinating electrification. Hardware alone retails around $3,500 before installation, which is real money — the bet is that one unified brain over every circuit is worth it. In early 2026 SPAN announced a partnership and $75M investment from Eaton, one of the biggest names in electrical equipment, aimed at distribution reach and driving costs down — a meaningful signal for long-term support, which matters for a product this cloud-connected. **Fits:** whole-home electrification, solar-plus-battery homes, and anyone whose panel needed replacing anyway. **Fights:** budgets, and homes that only ever wanted three circuits smart. ## Leviton: smart breakers in a familiar box Leviton approaches from the electrician's side: its load center looks and installs like a conventional panel, and you add its smart breakers only to the circuits you care about — the rest stay ordinary and cheap. Contractors know the format, installs are faster, and the entry cost is much lower than a full smart panel; you can start with two smart circuits and grow. Owner reports favor pairing the hub with wired Ethernet for reliability. The trade-off mirrors the [smart breakers vs smart panel](/smart-panel-vs-smart-breakers/) analysis: per-breaker costs climb as your ambitions grow, and whole-home orchestration is looser than a single-brain panel. **Fits:** targeted needs, staged budgets, new panels being installed anyway. **Fights:** sprawling smart-circuit wish lists, deep solar/battery orchestration. ## Lumin: keep your panel, add a smart layer Lumin's device is a smart subpanel that mounts beside your existing panel and takes over control of a dozen or so selected circuits. Nothing about your main panel changes — which is the whole appeal. It's the retrofit-friendly path to [load management](/smart-panel-vs-load-management/) and backup prioritization when your panel is healthy and replacing it would be waste. It's also notably battery-agnostic, pairing with many storage brands. **Fits:** healthy existing panels, battery retrofits, renters-of-their-own-panel situations where a full swap is overkill. **Fights:** homes that want every circuit smart, or whose panel is due for replacement regardless. ## Savant and Eaton: the modular and the incumbent Savant sells smart-circuit modules that bring monitoring and control to selected circuits inside existing panel setups — conceptually like Lumin's "keep your panel" philosophy with a strong smart-home integration story. Eaton, the electrical incumbent, sells AbleEdge smart breakers for compatible panels and now backs SPAN as well — so its DNA shows up in both the breaker route and the replacement route. If your electrician already works in an Eaton-compatible ecosystem, the breaker path there deserves a look. ## The cautionary tale: Schneider Pulse One more data point belongs in any honest overview: Schneider Electric — a giant of the industry — discontinued its Pulse smart panel. Owners of discontinued cloud-dependent hardware face uncertain app support and feature futures. The lesson isn't "avoid smart panels"; it's that vendor staying power is a real spec. Weigh company commitment (SPAN's Eaton backing, Leviton's and Eaton's scale, Lumin's installer network) the same way you weigh amps and app screenshots. ## How to choose without a ranking
Your situationArchitecture to shortlist
Panel is old/full and due for replacementFull replacement (SPAN)
Healthy panel, want a few smart circuitsSmart breakers (Leviton/Eaton)
Healthy panel, adding battery backupAdd-on subpanel (Lumin) or replacement
Deep smart-home integration is the pointSavant / SPAN
Just want visibility, not controlSkip panels — see a monitor
If the last row is you, an energy monitor delivers the insight for a tenth of the cost.
Prices move, models update, and incentives change — in the U.S., panel upgrades tied to efficiency projects may qualify for federal tax credits, which can meaningfully offset any of these routes. Verify current pricing and incentive eligibility for your specific project before deciding. ## Reading a quote like an insider Whichever architecture you shortlist, the quotes you receive will hide the decision-relevant numbers inside lump sums, so ask for the split explicitly: hardware, labor, permit and inspection, and any service-entrance or utility work. Two quotes for "a smart panel installed" can differ by thousands simply because one includes relocating the panel or upsizing the service entrance and the other doesn't. If a full-replacement quote (SPAN-style) looks shocking next to a smart-breaker quote (Leviton-style), check whether the replacement quote quietly includes work — a panel relocation, a mast replacement — that the breaker quote assumes away and that you might have needed regardless. Also ask each bidder two questions that reveal more than any brochure. First: "What happens to each feature if the vendor's cloud goes away?" — the answer maps which capabilities are local (protection always; often schedules) versus cloud-dependent (remote access, sometimes analytics), which is exactly the risk the Schneider Pulse story teaches. Second: "How many of these have you installed?" — smart panels reward installer familiarity, and an electrician fluent in your chosen ecosystem will configure load-management priorities correctly the first time instead of leaving defaults that never trip. Finally, sequence the purchase around your bigger projects. Buying the smart panel *during* the solar-and-battery install, or during the EV-charger project that triggered a load calculation, lets one permit, one inspection, and one electrician visit cover work that would cost more as three separate mobilizations. ## Quick answers **Which smart panel is objectively best?** None — the architectures serve different homes. Match replacement vs breakers vs add-on to your panel's health and your circuit count first; brands second. **Are these DIY installs?** No. All involve panel work that belongs to a licensed electrician, with permits and inspection. **What happened to Schneider's smart panel?** The Pulse was discontinued — a reminder to weigh vendor longevity as part of the purchase. **Do smart panels qualify for incentives?** Panel upgrades connected to qualifying electrification or efficiency work may be eligible for U.S. federal tax credits and utility rebates; confirm current rules for your project. ## The bottom line Shop the architecture before the brand. A due-for-replacement panel points to SPAN's all-in approach; a healthy panel with focused needs points to Leviton-style smart breakers or a Lumin-style add-on; visibility-only goals point away from panels entirely. Then weigh vendor staying power — the Schneider Pulse story is the industry telling you that support matters as much as specs. *Educational overview, not a tested ranking or professional advice. Product details, prices, and incentives change — verify before purchasing, and use a licensed electrician for all panel work. Model your loads in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

There is no single winner in smart panels, because the products on the market aren’t variations of one design — they’re genuinely different architectures aimed at different homes. Some replace your whole panel; some snap smart breakers into a load center; some bolt a smart subpanel beside what you already have. This guide walks through the real options — SPAN, Leviton, Lumin, Savant, and Eaton’s smart breakers — and explains why each design fits some situations better than others, so you can match the architecture to your home rather than shopping by brand buzz. We haven’t lab-tested these units, so you won’t find rankings here — just the structural differences that actually drive the decision.

Three architectures, not one product

The market splits into three design philosophies
🏠Full replacementSwap the whole panel for one with intelligence built into every circuit (SPAN).
🔧Smart breakers in a load centerA conventional-style panel that accepts smart breakers where you want them (Leviton; Eaton AbleEdge in compatible panels).
➕Add-on beside your panelKeep your existing panel; add a smart subpanel or modules for chosen circuits (Lumin, Savant).

Which architecture fits you is mostly decided by two facts about your house: whether your current panel is worth keeping, and how many circuits you truly want smart. A failing or full panel points to replacement; a healthy panel with focused needs points to the add-on or breaker routes. That single framing sorts most of the market.

SPAN: the all-in replacement

SPAN’s panel is the most software-forward take: replace the box entirely and every circuit becomes individually monitored and controllable from one app. It was the first panel certified to UL 3141, the power-control-systems standard, and it targets exactly the whole-home cases this site covers — fitting EV charging on tight service, prioritizing circuits during battery backup, and coordinating electrification. Hardware alone retails around $3,500 before installation, which is real money — the bet is that one unified brain over every circuit is worth it. In early 2026 SPAN announced a partnership and $75M investment from Eaton, one of the biggest names in electrical equipment, aimed at distribution reach and driving costs down — a meaningful signal for long-term support, which matters for a product this cloud-connected.

Fits: whole-home electrification, solar-plus-battery homes, and anyone whose panel needed replacing anyway. Fights: budgets, and homes that only ever wanted three circuits smart.

Leviton: smart breakers in a familiar box

Leviton approaches from the electrician’s side: its load center looks and installs like a conventional panel, and you add its smart breakers only to the circuits you care about — the rest stay ordinary and cheap. Contractors know the format, installs are faster, and the entry cost is much lower than a full smart panel; you can start with two smart circuits and grow. Owner reports favor pairing the hub with wired Ethernet for reliability. The trade-off mirrors the smart breakers vs smart panel analysis: per-breaker costs climb as your ambitions grow, and whole-home orchestration is looser than a single-brain panel.

Fits: targeted needs, staged budgets, new panels being installed anyway. Fights: sprawling smart-circuit wish lists, deep solar/battery orchestration.

Lumin: keep your panel, add a smart layer

Lumin’s device is a smart subpanel that mounts beside your existing panel and takes over control of a dozen or so selected circuits. Nothing about your main panel changes — which is the whole appeal. It’s the retrofit-friendly path to load management and backup prioritization when your panel is healthy and replacing it would be waste. It’s also notably battery-agnostic, pairing with many storage brands.

Fits: healthy existing panels, battery retrofits, renters-of-their-own-panel situations where a full swap is overkill. Fights: homes that want every circuit smart, or whose panel is due for replacement regardless.

Savant and Eaton: the modular and the incumbent

Savant sells smart-circuit modules that bring monitoring and control to selected circuits inside existing panel setups — conceptually like Lumin’s “keep your panel” philosophy with a strong smart-home integration story. Eaton, the electrical incumbent, sells AbleEdge smart breakers for compatible panels and now backs SPAN as well — so its DNA shows up in both the breaker route and the replacement route. If your electrician already works in an Eaton-compatible ecosystem, the breaker path there deserves a look.

The cautionary tale: Schneider Pulse

One more data point belongs in any honest overview: Schneider Electric — a giant of the industry — discontinued its Pulse smart panel. Owners of discontinued cloud-dependent hardware face uncertain app support and feature futures. The lesson isn’t “avoid smart panels”; it’s that vendor staying power is a real spec. Weigh company commitment (SPAN’s Eaton backing, Leviton’s and Eaton’s scale, Lumin’s installer network) the same way you weigh amps and app screenshots.

How to choose without a ranking

Your situationArchitecture to shortlist
Panel is old/full and due for replacementFull replacement (SPAN)
Healthy panel, want a few smart circuitsSmart breakers (Leviton/Eaton)
Healthy panel, adding battery backupAdd-on subpanel (Lumin) or replacement
Deep smart-home integration is the pointSavant / SPAN
Just want visibility, not controlSkip panels — see a monitor
If the last row is you, an energy monitor delivers the insight for a tenth of the cost.

Prices move, models update, and incentives change — in the U.S., panel upgrades tied to efficiency projects may qualify for federal tax credits, which can meaningfully offset any of these routes. Verify current pricing and incentive eligibility for your specific project before deciding.

Reading a quote like an insider

Whichever architecture you shortlist, the quotes you receive will hide the decision-relevant numbers inside lump sums, so ask for the split explicitly: hardware, labor, permit and inspection, and any service-entrance or utility work. Two quotes for “a smart panel installed” can differ by thousands simply because one includes relocating the panel or upsizing the service entrance and the other doesn’t. If a full-replacement quote (SPAN-style) looks shocking next to a smart-breaker quote (Leviton-style), check whether the replacement quote quietly includes work — a panel relocation, a mast replacement — that the breaker quote assumes away and that you might have needed regardless.

Also ask each bidder two questions that reveal more than any brochure. First: “What happens to each feature if the vendor’s cloud goes away?” — the answer maps which capabilities are local (protection always; often schedules) versus cloud-dependent (remote access, sometimes analytics), which is exactly the risk the Schneider Pulse story teaches. Second: “How many of these have you installed?” — smart panels reward installer familiarity, and an electrician fluent in your chosen ecosystem will configure load-management priorities correctly the first time instead of leaving defaults that never trip.

Finally, sequence the purchase around your bigger projects. Buying the smart panel during the solar-and-battery install, or during the EV-charger project that triggered a load calculation, lets one permit, one inspection, and one electrician visit cover work that would cost more as three separate mobilizations.

Quick answers

Which smart panel is objectively best? None — the architectures serve different homes. Match replacement vs breakers vs add-on to your panel’s health and your circuit count first; brands second.

Are these DIY installs? No. All involve panel work that belongs to a licensed electrician, with permits and inspection.

What happened to Schneider’s smart panel? The Pulse was discontinued — a reminder to weigh vendor longevity as part of the purchase.

Do smart panels qualify for incentives? Panel upgrades connected to qualifying electrification or efficiency work may be eligible for U.S. federal tax credits and utility rebates; confirm current rules for your project.

The bottom line

Shop the architecture before the brand. A due-for-replacement panel points to SPAN’s all-in approach; a healthy panel with focused needs points to Leviton-style smart breakers or a Lumin-style add-on; visibility-only goals point away from panels entirely. Then weigh vendor staying power — the Schneider Pulse story is the industry telling you that support matters as much as specs.

Educational overview, not a tested ranking or professional advice. Product details, prices, and incentives change — verify before purchasing, and use a licensed electrician for all panel work. Model your loads in the Sim-Lab.

Battery Mower vs Gas Mower: Which Is Better for Homeowners?
An honest side-by-side on power, runtime, cost per season, noise, maintenance and lifespan - and the yard sizes where each mower genuinely wins.

Ten years ago this comparison had an easy answer: gas for anyone serious, battery for tiny lawns and patient people. That answer is dead. Modern battery mowers cut real grass, self-propel up real hills, and outsell their reputation — while gas mowers still hold genuine advantages that battery marketing pretends away. What's left is an honest trade-off that resolves differently depending on your yard, your tolerance for maintenance, and how you feel about noise at 8 a.m. Let's take the categories one at a time, with real numbers where numbers exist, and end with the part most comparisons dodge: who should actually buy which. ## Power and cut quality For lawns, the gap has effectively closed. A modern brushless battery mower cuts typical suburban grass indistinguishably from gas — and battery torque delivery is instant, so many battery machines respond to thick patches by spinning up rather than bogging. Gas retains the edge at the extremes: tall wet overgrowth, fields cut twice a year, and all-day punishment favor combustion's indifference to load. If your "mowing" is occasionally rescuing a neglected half-acre of knee-high fescue, gas still earns it. If it's weekly cuts of a maintained lawn, the power question no longer separates them. ## Runtime and refueling — the real dividing line Here's the category that actually decides most purchases. A typical battery push mower cuts roughly a quarter to half an acre per charge depending on pack size and grass conditions; self-propelled models and hills eat into that. Gas runs as long as you keep pouring fuel. The mitigation is a second battery — swap and keep mowing — but that's real money, and it's how battery mowing scales in cost with lawn size. The honest framing: up to about a quarter acre, one pack does it with margin. Quarter to half acre, you want the bigger pack or a second one. Past a half acre to an acre, you're into premium large-deck battery machines or accepting mid-mow charging breaks — and past an acre, gas (or a battery riding mower, a different budget class) is still the pragmatic answer for most people. Match the machine to the yard, not the ideology. ## Cost: purchase, fuel, and the ten-year picture Purchase prices now overlap heavily — comparable battery and gas push mowers sit in the same few-hundred-dollar band, with battery kits carrying a modest premium that has shrunk every year (and battery models often include the charger and pack that constitute the real value). Operating cost favors battery clearly. Run the energy math: a typical battery mower pack stores around 0.3–0.5 kWh; at the current U.S. average residential electricity price of about 18.8 cents per kWh, a full charge costs roughly **6 to 9 cents** — call it a few dollars per season. A gas push mower burning roughly a quarter to half gallon per hour, at the recent national average of about $3.83 per gallon for East Coast regular gasoline pricing, runs **one to two dollars per mowing hour** — commonly $30–$60 per season, plus oil, plugs, filters and the annual tune-up (DIY parts money, or a real shop bill). Over a decade, battery's operating savings typically cover a large slice of a replacement pack. Which is the honest asterisk on battery ownership: the pack is a consumable. Lawn batteries generally give roughly three to five seasons (heavily influenced by storage habits — the [winter storage routine](/how-to-store-a-battery-mower-trimmer-and-blower-for-winter-homeowner-guide/) is where pack lifespans are won), and a replacement is a significant fraction of the mower's price. Budget for one pack replacement per mower lifetime and the comparison stays truthful in both directions.
A season of operating cost, typical quarter-acre lawn
Battery - electricity (~25 charges)
~$2
Battery - maintenance (blade care)
~$0-15
Gas - fuel (~25 mows)
~$30-60
Gas - oil, plug, filter, tune-up
~$25-80
Estimates from EIA average electricity and gasoline prices; your rates, lawn and habits will move these numbers.
## Maintenance, noise and the living-with-it factors **Maintenance** is a rout. Gas asks for oil changes, spark plugs, air filters, fuel stabilizer, carburetor attention when fuel goes stale, and the spring ritual. Battery asks for a scraped deck, a sharp blade, and disciplined pack care ([the whole routine](/how-to-maintain-battery-lawn-tools-homeowner-guide/) is minutes a week). The classic gas failure — sat all winter with fuel in it, now won't start — simply has no battery equivalent; the battery equivalent (pack stored badly) is fully preventable and free to prevent. **Noise** is the underrated one. Gas push mowers run loud enough that hearing protection is genuinely recommended for the operator; battery mowers run dramatically quieter — normal-conversation-adjacent — which means early Saturday mowing without neighborhood diplomacy, no ear muffs, and a chore that's simply less punishing. Add zero exhaust in your face, no gas can in the garage, no pull cord, push-button start, and the daily experience gap is wider than any spec sheet shows. **Lifespan** tilts gas on the chassis — well-maintained gas mowers famously run 10–15 years, and battery mower bodies increasingly match that — but battery ownership includes that mid-life pack purchase, while gas ownership includes a decade of small parts and labor. Roughly a wash, paid on different schedules. ## So who buys which?

Battery is your answer if...

  • Lawn is up to ~half an acre (most homeowners)
  • You hate maintenance, fumes, noise or pull cords
  • Early/late mowing matters in your neighborhood
  • You're already building a battery yard-tool platform
  • You'll store the pack properly (indoors, half charge)
VS

Gas still earns it if...

  • You're cutting an acre-plus with a push/self-propelled deck
  • Regular tall, wet or rough-field mowing
  • Long sessions where swap batteries get expensive
  • No indoor spot to winter lithium packs
  • You already own and happily maintain a good gas machine
That last gas bullet deserves respect: the greenest and cheapest mower is often the one already in your garage. The switch case is strongest when a gas mower dies anyway — replacing a working machine to save $50 a season is sentiment, not math. And if you do go battery, the platform question comes first (packs shared across a mower, trimmer and blower multiply the value — that logic lives in the [platform guide](/how-to-choose-the-right-battery-platform-homeowner-guide/)), and the pack-care habits from the [charging guide](/how-to-charge-cordless-tool-batteries-safely/) are what make the ownership math land on the good side. ## Quick answers **Are battery mowers as powerful as gas now?** For maintained lawns, effectively yes — modern brushless models cut equivalently. Gas keeps the edge only in sustained heavy overgrowth and rough-field work. **How much does it cost to charge a battery mower?** Pennies — roughly 6 to 9 cents per full charge at average U.S. electricity rates. A whole season of charging typically costs a few dollars against $30–60 in mower gas. **How long do battery mower batteries last?** Commonly three to five seasons, with winter storage habits the biggest variable. Budget one replacement pack across a mower's life for honest math. **What size lawn is too big for a battery mower?** Push models are comfortable to about a half acre (with an appropriately sized or second pack). Beyond an acre, gas or battery riding equipment becomes the practical answer. **Is a battery mower worth it over gas?** For most suburban lawns, yes — near-zero maintenance, dramatic noise reduction, no fuel handling, and lower running costs outweigh the pack-replacement cost. For large or rough properties, gas remains the honest pick. ## The bottom line Battery won the suburban lawn: equal cut, pennies to run, near-zero maintenance, and quiet enough to change when and how you mow. Gas kept the frontier: big acreage, rough growth, and run-all-day indifference. Size the decision to your actual yard, budget honestly for one battery replacement or a decade of gas-engine upkeep, and whichever side you land on, the machine will only be as good as its winter storage — which, conveniently, is the cheapest part to get right. *Educational content, not professional advice. Prices vary by region and season - EIA averages cited; check your local rates. Compare your own mowing costs directly in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

Cordless Power Tools: What Homeowners Actually Need
Skip the 40-tool fantasy kit. The cordless tools that earn their place in a real home, the ones that can wait, and the ones most people regret buying.

Walk the tool aisle and the message is that a proper homeowner owns forty cordless tools, each solving a problem you didn't know you had. Meanwhile the actual data of home life — the shelf that needs hanging, the toilet seat that needs swapping, the deck board that popped — gets handled by about four tools doing 95% of the work. This guide is the anti-catalog: what you genuinely need, ranked by how life actually generates tasks, plus the honest tier of "buy it when the project demands it" and the small hall of fame of purchases people regret. No "best" lists, no model shootouts — just which tools earn a spot, and why. (One decision does come before any of this: which battery platform you're buying into, because that choice outlives every individual tool. That's the [platform guide](/how-to-choose-the-right-battery-platform-homeowner-guide/) — read it first if you're starting from zero.) ## Tier one: the tools that do almost everything **A drill/driver.** The obvious one, and still underrated. Drilling holes and driving screws covers the overwhelming majority of household tasks, and a mid-range brushless drill from any major platform is more tool than most homes will ever exhaust. If you buy one cordless tool, it's this — usually in a kit with two batteries and the charger, which is the cheapest way batteries ever get bought. **An impact driver.** The tool people don't know they need until they use one, then can't give back. It drives screws and lags with rotational hammering that makes long fasteners feel effortless, doesn't cam out and strip heads the way drills do, and it's smaller and lighter than the drill it mostly replaces for fastening. Drill for holes, impact for screws — the two-tool combo is the real foundation, and combo kits price it accordingly. **A cordless vacuum (shop style).** Unsexy, constantly used. Drywall dust, the car, the workbench, the mystery pile — a hand or compact shop vac on your platform gets grabbed weekly forever. **Work light.** Nearly free as a bare tool, runs on the packs you already own, and transforms every under-sink, attic and breaker-panel job. The highest usefulness-per-dollar object in the whole ecosystem. ## Tier two: buy when the projects arrive
The when-you-need-it tier - and what triggers each
C
Circular sawThe first real lumber project - shelving, deck repair, plywood. The homeowner's primary saw.
R
Reciprocating sawDemolition, pruning, remodeling. Buy it the week you tear something out.
M
Multi-tool (oscillating)Trim cuts, grout, undercuts, the weird jobs. Earns cult loyalty once owned.
S
SanderFirst furniture refinish or paint-prep project. Random orbit is the default answer.
J
JigsawCurves and cutouts - countertop sinks, craft work. Waits patiently until then.
G
GrinderMetal, rust, masonry. Most homes need one eventually; few need it soon.
The discipline for this tier is simple and saves the most money: buy the bare tool (no battery) the week a real project demands it, running it on the packs you already own. That's the entire economic advantage of committing to one platform — tier-two tools cost roughly half as much bare, and your batteries already exist. What breaks people is buying tier two speculatively: the jigsaw purchased for someday sits in its box while its warranty quietly expires. Outdoor power gear — mower, trimmer, blower — is its own parallel track with its own logic and often its own higher-voltage platform; the [battery vs gas mower comparison](/battery-mower-vs-gas-mower-which-is-better-for-homeowners/) is the entry point to that decision, and it's genuinely separate from the shop-tool question. ## The regret list Every tool wall has one or two of these, purchased with love, used twice: **The 40-tool mega kit.** The bundle math looks unbeatable until you inventory what you'll use: four tools constantly, six occasionally, thirty never. Kits also lock every slot to that brand's budget line. Buy the two-tool combo plus bare tools as needed; it wins on both money and quality. **The specialty tool for one job.** The cordless caulk gun, the power scrubber, the inflator you bought instead of checking if your compressor... fine tools, real uses — for people with recurring versions of those jobs. For a one-time task, borrow, rent, or do it manually. **Duplicate capability in a second platform.** The sale-priced drill in a different color than your batteries is not a deal; it's a second charger, incompatible packs, and a permanent tax. Platform discipline is boring and correct. **Underpowered "compact" everything.** Compact tools are wonderful as a deliberate choice for light work and tight spaces. As the accidental default — because the kit was cheapest — they generate the "cordless tools are weak" myth one strained cut at a time. Match the tool class (and the battery size; a big saw on a tiny pack starves) to the actual work. ## Buying sense that isn't a model roundup A few durable rules that survive every product cycle. Brushless is worth it on anything you'll use regularly — cooler running, longer tool life, better runtime — while brushed is fine for the rarely-used. Batteries are the real spend over time, so kit purchases and battery-included promos are where the money math happens; tools are almost the accessory. Two batteries is the working minimum for any tool you rely on — one working, one charged — and how you treat those packs (charging habits, half-charge storage) determines the cost of ownership more than any purchase decision, which is why the [charging guide](/how-to-charge-cordless-tool-batteries-safely/) and [storage guide](/how-to-store-cordless-power-tools-safely-battery-first-approach/) are effectively part of the buying process. And warranty registration, the chore everyone skips: major brands cover tools for years, and the registration you didn't do is the free replacement you didn't get.
i
The whole strategy in one sentence: commit to one platform, buy the drill/impact combo kit with two batteries, add the vacuum and the light, then buy everything else bare on the day a project actually requires it.
## Quick answers **What's the first cordless tool a homeowner should buy?** A drill/driver — ideally as a drill-plus-impact-driver combo kit with two batteries, because that pairing covers the vast majority of household tasks and prices the batteries efficiently. **Do I really need both a drill and an impact driver?** Need is strong, but it's the upgrade with the highest payoff in the ecosystem: the impact drives fasteners dramatically better, and the drill stays free for holes. Combo kits exist because this pairing is the right answer for most people. **Are big cordless tool kits worth it?** Rarely. You pay for tools you'll never use, all locked to one brand's budget tier. The combo-plus-bare-tools path is cheaper over five years and yields better tools. **Is brushless worth the extra money?** For your frequently used tools, yes — more power, less heat, longer life. For the once-a-year tool, brushed is fine and the savings are real. **How many batteries do I need?** Two per platform minimum, sized to your biggest tool's appetite. Beyond that, add packs only when swapping mid-job becomes a real annoyance — and store the fleet at half-charge. ## The bottom line The honest homeowner kit is four tools deep — drill, impact driver, vacuum, light — with a bench of project-triggered bare-tool additions behind it and a hard rule against second platforms and speculative purchases. Buy the combo kit, register the warranty, treat the batteries right, and let real projects, not the tool aisle, decide what joins the wall next. Your money ends up in tools that get used, which is the only tool-buying metric that matters. *Educational content, not professional advice, and deliberately free of "best of" claims - match tools to your actual work. Curious what a growing tool wall costs to charge and run? Play with the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

Walk the tool aisle and the message is that a proper homeowner owns forty cordless tools, each solving a problem you didn’t know you had. Meanwhile the actual data of home life — the shelf that needs hanging, the toilet seat that needs swapping, the deck board that popped — gets handled by about four tools doing 95% of the work. This guide is the anti-catalog: what you genuinely need, ranked by how life actually generates tasks, plus the honest tier of “buy it when the project demands it” and the small hall of fame of purchases people regret. No “best” lists, no model shootouts — just which tools earn a spot, and why. (One decision does come before any of this: which battery platform you’re buying into, because that choice outlives every individual tool. That’s the platform guide — read it first if you’re starting from zero.)

Tier one: the tools that do almost everything

A drill/driver. The obvious one, and still underrated. Drilling holes and driving screws covers the overwhelming majority of household tasks, and a mid-range brushless drill from any major platform is more tool than most homes will ever exhaust. If you buy one cordless tool, it’s this — usually in a kit with two batteries and the charger, which is the cheapest way batteries ever get bought.

An impact driver. The tool people don’t know they need until they use one, then can’t give back. It drives screws and lags with rotational hammering that makes long fasteners feel effortless, doesn’t cam out and strip heads the way drills do, and it’s smaller and lighter than the drill it mostly replaces for fastening. Drill for holes, impact for screws — the two-tool combo is the real foundation, and combo kits price it accordingly.

A cordless vacuum (shop style). Unsexy, constantly used. Drywall dust, the car, the workbench, the mystery pile — a hand or compact shop vac on your platform gets grabbed weekly forever.

Work light. Nearly free as a bare tool, runs on the packs you already own, and transforms every under-sink, attic and breaker-panel job. The highest usefulness-per-dollar object in the whole ecosystem.

Tier two: buy when the projects arrive

The when-you-need-it tier - and what triggers each
C
Circular sawThe first real lumber project - shelving, deck repair, plywood. The homeowner's primary saw.
R
Reciprocating sawDemolition, pruning, remodeling. Buy it the week you tear something out.
M
Multi-tool (oscillating)Trim cuts, grout, undercuts, the weird jobs. Earns cult loyalty once owned.
S
SanderFirst furniture refinish or paint-prep project. Random orbit is the default answer.
J
JigsawCurves and cutouts - countertop sinks, craft work. Waits patiently until then.
G
GrinderMetal, rust, masonry. Most homes need one eventually; few need it soon.

The discipline for this tier is simple and saves the most money: buy the bare tool (no battery) the week a real project demands it, running it on the packs you already own. That’s the entire economic advantage of committing to one platform — tier-two tools cost roughly half as much bare, and your batteries already exist. What breaks people is buying tier two speculatively: the jigsaw purchased for someday sits in its box while its warranty quietly expires.

Outdoor power gear — mower, trimmer, blower — is its own parallel track with its own logic and often its own higher-voltage platform; the battery vs gas mower comparison is the entry point to that decision, and it’s genuinely separate from the shop-tool question.

The regret list

Every tool wall has one or two of these, purchased with love, used twice:

The 40-tool mega kit. The bundle math looks unbeatable until you inventory what you’ll use: four tools constantly, six occasionally, thirty never. Kits also lock every slot to that brand’s budget line. Buy the two-tool combo plus bare tools as needed; it wins on both money and quality.

The specialty tool for one job. The cordless caulk gun, the power scrubber, the inflator you bought instead of checking if your compressor… fine tools, real uses — for people with recurring versions of those jobs. For a one-time task, borrow, rent, or do it manually.

Duplicate capability in a second platform. The sale-priced drill in a different color than your batteries is not a deal; it’s a second charger, incompatible packs, and a permanent tax. Platform discipline is boring and correct.

Underpowered “compact” everything. Compact tools are wonderful as a deliberate choice for light work and tight spaces. As the accidental default — because the kit was cheapest — they generate the “cordless tools are weak” myth one strained cut at a time. Match the tool class (and the battery size; a big saw on a tiny pack starves) to the actual work.

Buying sense that isn’t a model roundup

A few durable rules that survive every product cycle. Brushless is worth it on anything you’ll use regularly — cooler running, longer tool life, better runtime — while brushed is fine for the rarely-used. Batteries are the real spend over time, so kit purchases and battery-included promos are where the money math happens; tools are almost the accessory. Two batteries is the working minimum for any tool you rely on — one working, one charged — and how you treat those packs (charging habits, half-charge storage) determines the cost of ownership more than any purchase decision, which is why the charging guide and storage guide are effectively part of the buying process. And warranty registration, the chore everyone skips: major brands cover tools for years, and the registration you didn’t do is the free replacement you didn’t get.

Emporia vs Sense: Two Ways to Watch Your Home Energy
Emporia measures every circuit with physical sensors; Sense infers devices from signatures. How the two philosophies differ and which fits your goals.

Emporia and Sense are the two names homeowners meet first when they go looking for an energy monitor, and the comparison is genuinely interesting because they attack the same problem with opposite philosophies. **Emporia** measures: you clamp a physical current sensor (CT) onto each circuit you care about, and the app reports exactly what each one draws — deterministic, labeled, immediate. **Sense** infers: it clamps just two sensors on your service mains, samples the incoming waveform about a million times a second, and uses machine learning to recognize individual appliances by their electrical signatures. One is bookkeeping; the other is detective work. Neither has been through our own lab, so this is an education in the two approaches — their genuine advantages, their honest failure modes, and which type of homeowner each suits. ## Two philosophies in one picture

Emporia (circuit measurement)

  • CT sensor per circuit — up to 16 branch CTs
  • You label circuits; data is exact from day one
  • ~±2% accuracy against the utility meter
  • Core features work without a subscription
VS

Sense (signature detection)

  • Two CTs on the mains — minimal wiring
  • ML learns devices over weeks from waveforms
  • Polished app; "device discovered" moments
  • Detection is probabilistic, not guaranteed
The hardware difference drives everything downstream. Emporia's install is fussier — sixteen sensor wires routed inside an already-crowded panel takes patience or an electrician's hour — but the payoff is data with no interpretation layer: the circuit you labeled "EV charger" reports the EV charger, full stop. Sense installs in a fraction of the time (two clamps, one connection), but the interpretation happens in software, and software has to *earn* its picture of your home over weeks of learning. ## Where signature detection shines and where it stalls Sense's party trick is real: turn on the toaster and a bubble can pop up naming it, because a toaster's resistive signature is distinctive. Appliances with crisp on/off signatures — kettles, resistive dryers, well pumps — are its home turf. Where it struggles is the modern home's growing gray zone: variable-speed motors, inverter-driven HVAC and heat pumps, LED lighting, and electronics that sip continuously rather than switching cleanly. Users commonly report a large chunk of usage pooled in "Other" and "Always On" buckets that detection never resolves, and there's no way to hand-train a stubborn device. If your big loads are inverter-based — increasingly true in exactly the electrified homes reading this site — expect more gray. ## Why circuit measurement usually wins for action
From data to a decision
Question: "Why did my bill jump $60?"
Emporia path:Open app → circuits ranked by kWh → "EV charger + water heater did it" → shift charging to off-peak. Minutes.
Sense path:Check detected devices → hope the culprits were learned → if they're in "Other," you're inferring from the total curve.
The pattern:Deterministic circuit data converts to specific actions; probabilistic detection sometimes does.
This is why EV owners in particular tend to land on Emporia: dedicate one CT to the charger's breaker and every kWh of charging is measured directly — the exact use case behind our [monitor vs smart panel](/smart-panel-vs-home-energy-monitor/) guidance. Solar households can also equip either system for production monitoring; Emporia handles net metering on its main CTs, and Sense sells a solar configuration. ## The honest case for Sense None of this makes Sense the wrong buy. Its install is genuinely easier — meaningful if you're paying an electrician hourly or your panel has no room for sixteen more wires. Its app is widely praised as the more polished experience. And the discovery experience is fun in a way spreadsheets aren't: many owners enjoy the game of identifying each newly detected device. If your goal is engagement and a live picture of "what's my house doing right now," and you treat device detection as a bonus rather than the product, Sense delivers a premium version of that. ## Match the tool to the goal
Your goalBetter philosophy
Explain and cut a specific billEmporia — labeled circuits
Track EV charging cost exactlyEmporia — dedicated CT
Minimal panel work, fastest installSense — two clamps
Enjoy discovery & a polished appSense
Inverter HVAC / heat pump homeEmporia — signatures blur
Whichever you choose, remember the finding from our [monitoring overview](/smart-energy-monitors-what-they-do-and-why-they-matter-2026-guide/): most of the savings come from visibility plus behavior change, and both products deliver visibility. The philosophies differ in *how confidently you can act* on what you see. ## Setup and living with each, honestly The first week with each device tells you what ownership is like. Emporia's week one is front-loaded work, then instant clarity: the install is the project (routing up to sixteen CT leads through a crowded panel takes an unhurried hour), but the moment power returns, every labeled circuit reports real numbers. There is no waiting period and nothing to train. The ongoing experience is spreadsheet-flavored — rankings, graphs, exports — which is exactly what bill-cutters want and tinkerers tolerate. Sense's week one is the opposite shape: twenty minutes of hardware, then a quiet stretch while the model listens. Detections arrive over days and weeks — the kettle early, the dryer eventually, the variable-speed pool pump possibly never — and each arrival is a small event the app celebrates. Owners consistently describe the early period as delightful and the plateau as the test of patience: detection slows, some devices stay stubbornly inside "Other," and there's no way to hand-label a load the model won't learn. Whether that bothers you depends entirely on whether you bought it for the discovery experience or for accounting. One practical note for both: these devices live or die by Wi-Fi reach into a metal box, often in a garage or basement corner. Check signal at the panel before install day — a weak link there causes more support tickets than any sensor issue. ## Quick answers **Which is more accurate?** For per-circuit numbers, direct measurement wins by construction — Emporia's CTs read the wire itself (~±2%). Sense's whole-home total is solid; its per-device split is an estimate. **Does either require a subscription?** Emporia's core monitoring doesn't. Check current terms for both before buying, as premium tiers exist. **Can I install them myself?** Both involve opening a live service panel. Sense is the shorter job, but if you're not fully comfortable, hire an electrician for either. **Do they work with solar?** Yes — both offer solar monitoring configurations; Emporia's net metering runs on its main CTs. **Can I run both?** Nothing prevents it — some enthusiasts pair Sense on the mains with a handful of dedicated CTs elsewhere — but for one budget, pick the philosophy that matches your goal rather than buying twice. ## The bottom line Buy Emporia if you want to *account* — exact, labeled, per-circuit numbers you can act on immediately, especially with an EV or inverter-heavy loads. Buy Sense if you want to *observe* — the easiest install and most engaging live picture, with device detection as a delightful maybe rather than a promise. Measurement versus inference is the whole decision; pick the philosophy that matches what you'll actually do with the data. *Educational comparison based on manufacturer documentation and owner reports, not our own lab testing. Product details change — verify current specs and pricing. Panel work belongs to a licensed electrician. Estimate your usage in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

How Much a Level 2 Charger Adds to Your Electric Bill
How much power a Level 2 charger draws, what it adds to your bill, why the wall draws more than the battery stores, and kW, kWh, amps, and volts explained.

A common worry before installing a home charger is that it will make the electric bill balloon, and the reassuring reality is that a Level 2 charger adds a predictable, moderate amount, typically 25 to 35 percent to a household's electricity use for average driving, or roughly 40 to 70 dollars a month. A Level 2 charger is a large appliance, but it only costs money while it is actually charging, and how much it draws follows simple electrical math. This guide explains how much power a Level 2 charger uses, what it adds to your bill, why the wall always draws a bit more than reaches the battery, and the basic units, kW, kWh, amps, and volts, that make it all make sense. ## The units, briefly
Volts and ampsLevel 2 runs on 240 volts (like a dryer). Amps set the current, commonly 32 or 48A for home chargers.
kW = powerVolts times amps gives power in kilowatts. A 240V, 48A charger draws about 11.5 kW.
kWh = energyPower times time. Charging at 11 kW for two hours adds about 22 kWh, and kWh is what you pay for.
The one distinction worth holding onto is power versus energy. Power, in kilowatts, is the rate a charger draws electricity at any instant; energy, in kilowatt-hours, is power accumulated over time, and energy is what your utility bills. A Level 2 charger draws high power, commonly 7 to 11 kW, but only while charging, so what hits your bill is the energy, kW times hours. This is the same kWh that drives every figure in our [cost-to-charge guide](/how-much-does-it-cost-to-charge-an-ev-at-home-2026-guide/). ## How much power a Level 2 charger draws Level 2 charging runs on a 240-volt circuit, the same voltage as an electric dryer or oven, and the current depends on the charger and circuit, typically 32 to 48 amps. Multiplying volts by amps gives power: a 240-volt, 40-amp setup delivers about 9.6 kW, and a 48-amp unit about 11.5 kW. At those rates a charger adds roughly 25 to 40 miles of range per hour, which is why Level 2 is the practical choice for home charging over the much slower Level 1. The draw is substantial while active, but it runs only for the hours needed to replace your driving, usually a few hours overnight. ## What it adds to your bill
Light driver~$25-40/month added
Average driver~$40-70/month added
Heavy driver~$90-140/month added
At national-average rates; off-peak charging roughly halves these.
Despite the high power draw, the monthly bill impact is moderate because you only pay for energy actually used. For average driving, a Level 2 charger raises a household's electricity use by roughly 25 to 35 percent, which lands near 40 to 70 dollars a month at national-average rates. Charging off-peak cuts that substantially, as our [time-of-use guide](/smart-charging-time-of-use-tou-2026-home-charging-optimization-guide/) shows. Importantly, Level 1 and Level 2 cost the same per kWh, since the electricity comes from the same meter; Level 2 is just faster, and slightly more efficient, not more expensive per unit of energy. ## Why the wall draws more than the battery
Charging is about 90 percent efficient on Level 2. Some energy becomes heat in the charger, the car's onboard converter, and the battery system. So the wall draws roughly 10 percent more kWh than the battery stores, and your bill reflects the wall figure.
Here is a subtlety that surprises careful budgeters: your car reports the kWh it added to the battery, but your utility bills the kWh pulled from the wall, and the two differ by about ten percent on a Level 2 charger. That gap is charging loss, energy converted to heat in the charger electronics, the onboard converter, and the battery management system. It is why the honest cost formula divides battery energy by efficiency, and why your bill runs slightly above a calculation based on the car's numbers, a point our [higher-than-expected guide](/why-your-ev-charging-cost-is-higher-than-expected-2026-guide/) also raises. Level 1 charging is a bit less efficient, near 85 percent. ## Putting it together Once the units click, the whole picture is simple: a Level 2 charger is a high-power, 240-volt appliance that draws 7 to 11 kW while active, runs a few hours a night, and adds a predictable 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving, less off-peak. It costs the same per kWh as slow charging but finishes far faster, and the wall draws about ten percent more energy than the battery keeps. There is nothing mysterious about the bill impact; it is your driving, converted to kWh, times your rate, which the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/) will compute from your own numbers. ## Level 1 versus Level 2 on the bill Since many new owners wonder whether the faster charger will cost more to run, it is worth settling directly: Level 1 and Level 2 charging cost essentially the same per kWh, because both pull from the same residential meter at the same rate. The difference is speed and a small efficiency edge, not price per unit of energy. Level 1, on a standard 120-volt outlet, trickles in a few miles of range per hour and is slightly less efficient at around 85 percent, so it wastes marginally more energy as heat. Level 2, on a 240-volt circuit, adds range several times faster and runs nearer 90 percent efficiency, so over time it actually costs a hair less for the same delivered energy. The reason to choose Level 2 is therefore convenience: it replaces a day's driving in a couple of overnight hours instead of struggling to keep up. The bill impact is set by how much you drive, not by which level you use, so the faster charger does not carry a running-cost penalty, only the one-time installation. ## Quick answers **How much power does a Level 2 charger use?** Commonly 7 to 11 kW while charging, from a 240-volt circuit at 32 to 48 amps. Only while actively charging. **What does it add to my bill?** Roughly 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving at average rates, less on an off-peak plan. **Is Level 2 more expensive per mile than Level 1?** No. Same rate per kWh from the same meter. Level 2 is just faster and slightly more efficient. **Why is my bill higher than the car's kWh figure?** Charging losses. The wall draws about ten percent more than the battery stores; your utility bills the wall. ## The bottom line A home Level 2 charger draws high power, 7 to 11 kW, but only while charging, so it adds a moderate and predictable 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving, roughly a 25 to 35 percent bump to a household's electricity use, and less on an off-peak plan. It costs the same per kWh as slow charging, just faster, and the wall draws about ten percent more energy than reaches the battery. Understand kW versus kWh and the bill holds no surprises. The one-time installation aside, a Level 2 charger is simply a faster way to buy the same cheap energy, and its monthly footprint on your bill is modest, predictable, and easy to shrink further by charging overnight. *Figures reflect mid-2026 US averages and typical home charger specs; they are educational, not a quote. Charger amperage, rates, and driving vary; use your own numbers. Estimate your cost in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/).*

How much power a Level 2 charger draws

Level 2 charging runs on a 240-volt circuit, the same voltage as an electric dryer or oven, and the current depends on the charger and circuit, typically 32 to 48 amps. Multiplying volts by amps gives power: a 240-volt, 40-amp setup delivers about 9.6 kW, and a 48-amp unit about 11.5 kW. At those rates a charger adds roughly 25 to 40 miles of range per hour, which is why Level 2 is the practical choice for home charging over the much slower Level 1. The draw is substantial while active, but it runs only for the hours needed to replace your driving, usually a few hours overnight.

What it adds to your bill

Light driver~$25-40/month added
Average driver~$40-70/month added
Heavy driver~$90-140/month added
At national-average rates; off-peak charging roughly halves these.

Despite the high power draw, the monthly bill impact is moderate because you only pay for energy actually used. For average driving, a Level 2 charger raises a household’s electricity use by roughly 25 to 35 percent, which lands near 40 to 70 dollars a month at national-average rates. Charging off-peak cuts that substantially, as our time-of-use guide shows. Importantly, Level 1 and Level 2 cost the same per kWh, since the electricity comes from the same meter; Level 2 is just faster, and slightly more efficient, not more expensive per unit of energy.

Why the wall draws more than the battery

Charging is about 90 percent efficient on Level 2. Some energy becomes heat in the charger, the car's onboard converter, and the battery system. So the wall draws roughly 10 percent more kWh than the battery stores, and your bill reflects the wall figure.

Here is a subtlety that surprises careful budgeters: your car reports the kWh it added to the battery, but your utility bills the kWh pulled from the wall, and the two differ by about ten percent on a Level 2 charger. That gap is charging loss, energy converted to heat in the charger electronics, the onboard converter, and the battery management system. It is why the honest cost formula divides battery energy by efficiency, and why your bill runs slightly above a calculation based on the car’s numbers, a point our higher-than-expected guide also raises. Level 1 charging is a bit less efficient, near 85 percent.

Putting it together

Once the units click, the whole picture is simple: a Level 2 charger is a high-power, 240-volt appliance that draws 7 to 11 kW while active, runs a few hours a night, and adds a predictable 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving, less off-peak. It costs the same per kWh as slow charging but finishes far faster, and the wall draws about ten percent more energy than the battery keeps. There is nothing mysterious about the bill impact; it is your driving, converted to kWh, times your rate, which the Sim-Lab will compute from your own numbers.

Level 1 versus Level 2 on the bill

Since many new owners wonder whether the faster charger will cost more to run, it is worth settling directly: Level 1 and Level 2 charging cost essentially the same per kWh, because both pull from the same residential meter at the same rate. The difference is speed and a small efficiency edge, not price per unit of energy. Level 1, on a standard 120-volt outlet, trickles in a few miles of range per hour and is slightly less efficient at around 85 percent, so it wastes marginally more energy as heat. Level 2, on a 240-volt circuit, adds range several times faster and runs nearer 90 percent efficiency, so over time it actually costs a hair less for the same delivered energy. The reason to choose Level 2 is therefore convenience: it replaces a day’s driving in a couple of overnight hours instead of struggling to keep up. The bill impact is set by how much you drive, not by which level you use, so the faster charger does not carry a running-cost penalty, only the one-time installation.

Quick answers

How much power does a Level 2 charger use? Commonly 7 to 11 kW while charging, from a 240-volt circuit at 32 to 48 amps. Only while actively charging.

What does it add to my bill? Roughly 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving at average rates, less on an off-peak plan.

Is Level 2 more expensive per mile than Level 1? No. Same rate per kWh from the same meter. Level 2 is just faster and slightly more efficient.

Why is my bill higher than the car’s kWh figure? Charging losses. The wall draws about ten percent more than the battery stores; your utility bills the wall.

The bottom line

A home Level 2 charger draws high power, 7 to 11 kW, but only while charging, so it adds a moderate and predictable 40 to 70 dollars a month for average driving, roughly a 25 to 35 percent bump to a household’s electricity use, and less on an off-peak plan. It costs the same per kWh as slow charging, just faster, and the wall draws about ten percent more energy than reaches the battery. Understand kW versus kWh and the bill holds no surprises. The one-time installation aside, a Level 2 charger is simply a faster way to buy the same cheap energy, and its monthly footprint on your bill is modest, predictable, and easy to shrink further by charging overnight.

Figures reflect mid-2026 US averages and typical home charger specs; they are educational, not a quote. Charger amperage, rates, and driving vary; use your own numbers. Estimate your cost in the Sim-Lab.

Cordless tools need less care than gas or corded ever did - but not zero. Vents, contacts, chucks and the safe wipe-down that keeps tools alive for years.

One of the quiet luxuries of going cordless is how much maintenance simply disappeared. No spark plugs, no carburetors, no fuel to stabilize, no brushes to swap on most modern tools, no cord to inspect. It's tempting to round that down to "zero maintenance" — and that's the mistake this guide exists to catch, because cordless tools do have a failure diet, and it's almost entirely one ingredient: dust, in three places. Dust in the motor vents cooks the electronics. Dust and grime on the battery contacts causes the flaky, intermittent behavior people misdiagnose as tool failure. Dust and resin in the chuck and moving bits makes good tools feel worn out. The whole homeowner maintenance program is defending those three fronts, plus a wipe-down method that doesn't do more harm than good. Fifteen minutes a month covers a whole tool wall. ## Front one: the vents (this is the big one) Every cordless tool cools its motor and electronics by pulling air through vent slots — which means every tool is also a small vacuum cleaner pointed at its own guts, running in the dustiest air in your house. Sanders, saws and grinders live inside their own dust plume; even a drill inhales plenty. As dust packs the vents and coats the internals, the tool runs hotter, and heat is what actually kills modern tools — brushless motors and their control boards fail from cumulative heat far more often than from mechanical wear. A tool that's started cutting out mid-job and recovering after a rest is very often just a tool that can't breathe (that symptom chain is walked in the [troubleshooting guide](/troubleshooting-cordless-tools-homeowner-safe-fixes/)). The fix costs two minutes: battery off, then vacuum the vents with a brush attachment, using a dry paintbrush to loosen packed dust from the slots. Vacuum beats compressed air here — a blast of air rams debris deeper into bearings, switches and boards, and turns the mess airborne besides. If you must use air (some packed housings only yield to it), short bursts, outdoors, from a few inches back, and never aimed to drive dust further in. Dusty-tool households — anyone who sands or cuts regularly — should treat vent care as an after-every-heavy-session habit, not a monthly one. ## Front two: the contact patch The battery-to-tool connection is the entire power system of a cordless tool, and it's exposed brass sliding together in dirty air. Oxide film, sawdust and grime here create resistance, and resistance shows up as everything people hate: intermittent cutouts, weak power, packs that "die" when the tool tilts, chargers that hesitate. The care routine is gentle by design — wipe both sets of contacts (tool and pack) with a clean dry cloth monthly; for visible grime or the dull gray film of oxidation, a cotton swab lightly dampened with isopropyl alcohol, then fully dry before reassembly. Two nevers: never sand or file contacts (you'll strip the plating and buy yourself permanent corrosion), and never spray anything at them — liquids go on the swab, not the tool. While you're there, a two-second look at the battery rails and latch keeps the mechanical connection honest too; a cracked latch makes a pack ride loose forever after. ## Front three: chucks, collets and the business end
PartSymptom when dirtyThe fix
Drill chuckWon't grip bits, gritty rotation, hard to openOpen fully, brush out, drop of light oil at the jaws, cycle, wipe dry
Impact driver colletBits stick or wobblePull collar, clean hex socket with swab, whisper of dry lube
Saw blade mount / guardGuard sluggish, blade wobbleBattery out, brush pitch and dust from guard pivot and arbor
Sander pad and dust portWeak dust pickup, pad swirlsClear the port, replace clogged pads, empty the box before full
Blades and bits themselves"Dull" cutting, burning woodClean the pitch off before assuming dull - a filthy blade cuts like a dead one
That last row saves real money. Saw blades and router bits caked in resin cut hot and slow, and most "this blade is done" verdicts are really "this blade is dirty." A soak in blade cleaner or degreaser, a brass-brush scrub, dry, and a corrosion-inhibiting wipe brings back the cut for a fraction of sharpening — and reveals which edges are genuinely worn. Lubrication across all of this stays minimal and dry-leaning: a drop of light machine oil on chuck jaws, dry PTFE where manuals allow it, and excess always wiped away, because wet lubricant in a dusty shop is a grit magnet you installed on purpose. ## The safe wipe-down (and the solvent trap) Housings and handles get grimy, and the correct cleaning is duller than the internet suggests: battery out first, dry debris brushed and vacuumed off, then a cloth barely dampened with mild soapy water, then dry. Nothing sprayed at the tool, nothing dripping near vents, switches or the battery interface — a tool should never be wet, only wiped.
!
Keep solvents away from tool bodies. Brake cleaner, acetone and aggressive degreasers cloud and crack plastic housings, eat the rubber overmold, erase safety labels, and can strip factory lubrication out of mechanisms if they wick inside. Their legitimate territory is bare steel - blades, bits, and hand tools - never housings, and never anywhere near the battery or its contacts.
Rubber overmolded grips that have gone sticky with age and sunscreen respond to soapy water and patience; once overmold turns permanently tacky, it's cosmetic aging, not dirt, and no chemical fixes it without making it worse. ## The monthly quarter-hour, and what maintenance can't fix Bundle it: one evening a month, the battery comes off each tool, vents get vacuumed, contacts get wiped, the chuck gets a brush, the housing gets a damp-cloth pass, and everything gets a ten-second inspection — cracked housings, wobbling chucks, guards that don't snap back, and anything that smells electrical. That inspection line matters because maintenance has a boundary: grinding gearboxes, burnt smells, heavy sparking and dead control boards aren't cleaning problems, and the honest next steps (warranty first, bare-tool replacement second, never opening a battery) are laid out in the [troubleshooting guide](/troubleshooting-cordless-tools-homeowner-safe-fixes/). Maintenance also can't outrun bad storage — a spotless tool with a pack that froze all winter is still a dead tool in April, which is why this routine pairs with the [battery-first storage approach](/how-to-store-cordless-power-tools-safely-battery-first-approach/), and why the pack-care half of the story (charging habits, temperature, the half-charge rule) lives in the [safe charging guide](/how-to-charge-cordless-tool-batteries-safely/). Lawn tools add grass, sap and weather to the diet and get their own regimen in the [battery lawn tool maintenance guide](/how-to-maintain-battery-lawn-tools-homeowner-guide/). ## Quick answers **How often should cordless tools be cleaned?** A monthly quarter-hour for the wall, plus vent-vacuuming after any heavy sanding or cutting session. Dusty-work households should think per-session, not per-month. **Can I use compressed air to clean power tools?** Vacuum first, always — air drives dust deeper into bearings and switches. If air is unavoidable, short bursts from distance, outdoors, angled to push debris out rather than in. **What should I clean battery contacts with?** A dry cloth for routine care; isopropyl alcohol on a swab for grime or oxide film, dried fully before use. Never abrasives, never sprays. **Why does my "dull" saw blade burn wood?** It's probably coated in pitch, not dull. Clean it with blade cleaner and a brass brush before paying for sharpening or replacement. **Do brushless tools need any maintenance?** Less than brushed — no brushes to replace — but the dust-and-heat story is identical. Vents, contacts and chucks still want the routine; heat still kills the electronics. ## The bottom line Cordless maintenance is dust defense at three gates — vents so the tool can breathe, contacts so power flows clean, chucks so the work end stays true — wrapped in a damp-cloth wipe that never gets the tool wet and never meets a solvent. Fifteen minutes a month, battery off first, vacuum over air, and the inspection eye open for the failures cleaning can't fix. Do that, and the tools become what they were advertised to be: the gear you never think about. *Educational content, not professional advice. Always remove the battery before any cleaning, and follow your tool manuals. Wondering what your shop draws in power? Check the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

How Much It Costs to Charge an EV at Home
The real cost of home EV charging in 2026, with the simple formula, worked examples at average rates, and the levers that move your number up or down.

The honest answer to what it costs to charge an EV at home is that most drivers spend somewhere around 35 to 60 dollars a month, but the number that matters is your own, and it comes from a formula simple enough to do on a napkin. Home charging cost is just the electricity you add multiplied by your rate, nudged up about ten percent for charging losses. Everything else, the model comparisons, the monthly estimates, the savings-versus-gas headlines, is that one equation dressed up. This guide gives you the formula, works it at the mid-2026 national-average electricity rate, and then shows exactly which levers move your cost up or down, so you leave able to estimate your own bill rather than trusting a stranger on the internet. ## The formula, once
Cost to charge = kWh added × your rate per kWh. To go from miles to kWh, divide miles by your EV efficiency in miles per kWh. Add roughly 10 percent for Level 2 charging losses, since not all the power from the wall reaches the battery.
Two numbers drive everything: your electricity rate and your car efficiency. Your rate is printed on your utility bill as the price per kWh. Your efficiency, in miles per kWh, comes off your dashboard or the EPA sticker, and for most EVs it lands between 3 and 4 miles per kWh, with efficient sedans nearer 4 and trucks nearer 2. Cost per mile is simply your rate divided by your efficiency. That single ratio is the cleanest way to compare any EV to any gas car, and it is what the [Sim-Lab charging calculator](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/) computes for your exact inputs. ## What the national average actually is As of mid-2026, the US average residential electricity rate is about 18.8 cents per kWh according to federal energy data, and it has been climbing, up roughly 7 percent over the prior year. But an average almost nobody pays: rates run near 12 cents in the cheapest states like North Dakota and past 40 cents in Hawaii, with California around 35 cents. So the same EV can cost three or four times as much to charge depending only on where you plug in. This is why we distrust any single headline figure and teach the formula instead, because your state rate changes the answer more than your choice of car does.
Scenario (3.5 mi/kWh EV)RateCost per mile
Cheap-rate state~12¢/kWh~3.4¢/mi
National average~18.8¢/kWh~5.4¢/mi
High-rate state~35¢/kWh~10¢/mi
Off-peak TOU charging~9¢/kWh~2.6¢/mi
## A worked monthly estimate Put real numbers to it. The average American drives roughly 1,000 to 1,200 miles a month. Take 1,100 miles in a 3.5 mile-per-kWh EV: that is about 314 kWh into the battery, or roughly 350 kWh from the wall after losses. At the national-average 18.8 cents, that is about 66 dollars a month. Drop to an off-peak rate near 9 cents and the same driving falls to roughly 32 dollars. Push to a 35-cent high-rate state and it climbs past 120 dollars. The 35-to-60-dollar figure you see quoted assumes average rates and average miles; your mileage and your rate slide it in either direction, which our [per-month guide](/how-much-it-costs-to-charge-an-ev-per-month-real-bills-explained/) breaks down against real bills. ## The levers that move your number
What raises and lowers your cost
Off-peak TOU chargingBiggest lever down
Your state rateBiggest lever, either way
Vehicle efficiencyTrucks cost more per mile
Winter coldRaises kWh per mile
Public DC fast charging2-3x home cost
The single biggest lever you control is when you charge. Many utilities offer time-of-use plans where overnight power costs far less than daytime, often 40 to 60 percent less, and since most home charging happens while you sleep, shifting to off-peak is close to free money, a topic our [time-of-use guide](/smart-charging-time-of-use-tou-2026-home-charging-optimization-guide/) covers in full. The other big one is avoiding public DC fast charging for daily needs, since at 30 to 50 cents per kWh it costs two to three times what home charging does. Vehicle efficiency and winter cold move the number too, just less dramatically. ## Home versus public, briefly Because roughly 80 percent of EV charging happens at home for most owners, your real-world cost tracks the home rate closely even if you occasionally fast-charge on a road trip. Public Level 2 and DC fast stations carry overhead that home charging does not, so they cost more per kWh, sometimes enough that a heavy fast-charging habit erases much of the EV savings. The practical takeaway is to charge at home overnight for daily driving and treat fast charging as a road-trip tool, which our [home-versus-public comparison](/home-charging-vs-public-charging-cost-comparison-2026-guide/) quantifies. ## How it compares to gas The comparison that motivates most buyers is stark at average prices. With gas near 3.80 dollars a gallon in a 30-mpg car, gasoline runs about 13 cents a mile, while home EV charging at the national-average rate runs about 5 cents a mile, roughly a third to half the cost. Over 12,000 miles a year that difference is commonly 700 to 1,200 dollars saved on fuel, more in cheap-electricity states and less in the priciest ones. Hawaii is the rare place the math tightens, though its gas is also expensive. Everywhere else on the mainland, home charging wins clearly. ## A note on rising rates One reason to compute your own number rather than trust an old estimate is that electricity prices have been climbing steadily. National-average residential rates rose roughly 7 percent over the past year and about 25 percent over four years, driven by grid modernization, natural gas prices, and surging demand from data centers and electrification. That trend means a charging cost you calculated two years ago understates today's, and it will likely keep drifting upward. The practical response is not alarm but habit: charging off-peak insulates you from much of the increase, since off-peak rates tend to stay low even as flat rates rise, and it locks in the cheapest window your utility offers. Recompute your cost yearly with your current rate, and lean on off-peak charging to keep the number down as the broader price of electricity continues to rise. ## Quick answers **What is the one-line answer?** Around 35 to 60 dollars a month at average rates and average miles, but compute your own: kWh added times your rate. **What rate should I use?** The price per kWh on your own bill. The national average near 18.8 cents is only a placeholder until you find yours. **How do I cut it most?** Shift charging to off-peak hours on a time-of-use plan; that is the biggest lever most homeowners control. **Does fast charging count?** Only for the sessions you use it. At home you pay your residential rate; public DC fast charging costs two to three times more. ## The bottom line Home EV charging costs whatever your electricity rate times your energy use says it does, and for average rates and average driving that lands near 35 to 60 dollars a month, a third to half the cost of gasoline. Your state rate is the biggest variable, and off-peak charging is the biggest lever you control. Skip the headline figures, find your rate on your bill, and run the formula, or let the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/) run it for you. *Figures reflect mid-2026 US averages from public energy data and are educational, not a quote for your situation. Electricity rates vary widely by state and change over time; use your own bill. Estimate your cost in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/).*
