Tools and Garage: The Complete Homeowner Guide
The hub for cordless tools, battery care, charging safety and workshop setup - what to own, how to keep it running, and how to fix it when it stops.

Somewhere in your garage right now there's probably a drill with a dead battery, a charger with a blinking light you've never decoded, and a mower you're hoping starts next spring. That's normal. Battery tools have quietly taken over the homeowner workshop — they're better than the gas and corded stuff they replaced in almost every way — but they brought a new set of skills with them, and nobody handed out the manual. How you charge, store and winter a lithium-ion pack now matters more than how you oil a hinge ever did. This hub is that manual. It's organized the way problems actually show up: something won't work, something needs charging or storing, you're buying into a platform, or you're setting up the space itself. Each section points to a full guide. ## When something won't work Start here, because this is why most people land on a page like this. Nine times out of ten the "broken" thing isn't broken. If a **tool** runs rough, stops under load, or won't start at all, work through the [cordless tool troubleshooting guide](/troubleshooting-cordless-tools-homeowner-safe-fixes/) — it separates the two-minute fixes (contacts, battery seating, overheat lockouts) from the genuine failures, and it's honest about which repairs a homeowner should not attempt. If a **battery won't charge**, the charger's blinking light is trying to tell you something specific. Most "dead" packs are actually in a temperature lockout or a protective deep-sleep, not dead. The [battery won't charge troubleshooting guide](/why-your-battery-wont-charge-troubleshooting-guide-for-lawn-tools/) decodes the light patterns brand by brand, and the [safe battery revival guide](/how-to-revive-a-dead-lithium-battery-safe-methods-only/) covers what you can legitimately do about a pack the charger refuses — and why the jump-start trick all over YouTube is the one method to skip. ## Batteries: the part that actually needs you Here's the mental shift battery tools require: the tool is durable, the battery is perishable, and almost everything that shortens a battery's life happens while you're not using it. Left on the charger for weeks. Stored dead. Stored full. Frozen in the garage all winter. Baked on a sunny windowsill in July.
What kills lithium-ion tool batteries (roughly in order)
Stored dead (deep discharge)
Worst
Heat - hot garage, sun, hot charging
Severe
Living on the charger at 100%
High
Charging below freezing
High
Normal use and honest wear
Low
The counter-moves are simple and they're spelled out in three guides. [How to charge cordless tool batteries safely](/how-to-charge-cordless-tool-batteries-safely/) covers the rules that matter: room-temperature charging (roughly 40–104°F), original chargers only, off the charger when done. [How to store cordless power tools safely](/how-to-store-cordless-power-tools-safely-battery-first-approach/) makes the case for the battery-first approach — packs out of the tools, at 40–50% charge, somewhere that doesn't freeze or bake. And the [battery tool safety guide](/battery-tool-safety-for-homeowners-essential-guide/) covers the part nobody wants to think about: what thermal runaway is, how to spot a failing pack (swelling, heat, odor), and where damaged batteries actually go — hint: never the trash, and not the retail drop-off bin either. If you're ready to give charging a proper home instead of a random outlet, the [charging station setup guide](/how-to-set-up-a-charging-station-for-cordless-tools/) covers the wall, and the [fire-resistant battery cabinet guide](/how-to-build-a-fire-resistant-battery-storage-cabinet/) covers containment for households with a lot of packs. ## Keeping tools alive Maintenance on cordless tools is refreshingly small — no oil, no spark plugs, no carburetors — but it's not zero, and the few tasks that exist are the difference between a drill that lasts fifteen years and one that cooks its motor in three. The [cordless tool maintenance guide](/cordless-tool-maintenance-for-homeowners/) covers the whole loop: vents, contacts, chucks, and the safe wipe-down methods that don't push liquid into a motor. The [air compressor guide](/how-to-clean-and-maintain-your-air-compressor-filters-tanks-and-hoses/) handles the one shop machine that can rust itself to death from the inside if you never drain the tank. Outdoor power gear gets its own pair: [how to maintain battery lawn tools](/how-to-maintain-battery-lawn-tools-homeowner-guide/) for the in-season care, and [how to store a battery mower, trimmer and blower for winter](/how-to-store-a-battery-mower-trimmer-and-blower-for-winter-homeowner-guide/) for the October ritual that decides whether spring starts with mowing or with troubleshooting. ## Buying in: platforms before tools The single most consequential decision in cordless tools isn't which drill to buy — it's which battery platform to marry. Batteries and chargers only work within one brand's system, they're where the real money goes, and switching later means abandoning that investment. Before any individual tool purchase, read [how to choose the right battery platform](/how-to-choose-the-right-battery-platform-homeowner-guide/); if you're weighing the big shop brands head-to-head, the [DeWalt vs Milwaukee vs Ryobi vs Makita ecosystem comparison](/how-to-compare-power-tool-ecosystems-dewalt-vs-milwaukee-vs-ryobi-vs-makita/) lays out what each system is genuinely good at without pretending there's one right answer. Then, and only then, decide what to actually buy. [Cordless power tools homeowners actually need](/cordless-power-tools-what-homeowners-actually-need/) is the anti-shopping-list: the handful of tools that earn their place, the ones that can wait, and the ones most people regret. On the yard side, [battery mower vs gas mower](/battery-mower-vs-gas-mower-which-is-better-for-homeowners/) settles the biggest single question with real trade-offs instead of cheerleading. ## The space itself A workshop that fights you gets abandoned, and most homeowner workshops fail on the same three things: not enough light where the cutting happens, no dedicated home for charging so batteries end up on whatever surface was empty, and a floor plan that puts the person between the tool and the door. [How to use power tools safely in a small garage](/how-to-use-power-tools-safely-in-a-small-garage-space-lighting-and-workflow-tips/) covers the layout, lighting and workflow habits that make a one-car garage work like a shop — where the cutting happens, where the batteries live, what needs light, and the handful of habits that prevent the classic small-space accidents. ## Quick answers **What charge level should batteries be stored at?** Around 40–50%, off the charger, somewhere that stays above freezing. Full and empty are both bad places for a lithium-ion pack to sit. **Why won't my battery charge after winter in the garage?** Usually cold lockout (warm it indoors a couple of hours) or deep discharge from sitting — the charger reads a deeply drained pack as defective. The [won't-charge guide](/why-your-battery-wont-charge-troubleshooting-guide-for-lawn-tools/) walks the whole diagnosis. **Are battery tools worth it over gas and corded?** For nearly all homeowner use, yes — instant start, no fuel, little maintenance. The honest exceptions are all-day heavy work and a few big-acreage yard cases, covered in the [mower comparison](/battery-mower-vs-gas-mower-which-is-better-for-homeowners/). **Do I really have to stay on one battery brand?** Within a system, yes — packs aren't cross-compatible, and adapter workarounds void warranties. That's why the [platform decision](/how-to-choose-the-right-battery-platform-homeowner-guide/) comes before the first tool. **Where do old tool batteries go?** Free drop-off bins at major hardware retailers handle healthy packs (tape the terminals). Swollen or damaged packs are different — those go through household hazardous waste. Details in the [safety guide](/battery-tool-safety-for-homeowners-essential-guide/). ## The bottom line Battery tools rewrote the homeowner workshop, and the skills that matter now are battery skills: charge at room temperature, store at half-charge, keep packs out of the heat and cold, decode the blinking light before declaring anything dead, and treat a swollen pack as the hazard it is. Somewhere in your garage right now there’s probably a drill with a dead battery, a charger with a blinking light you’ve never decoded, and a mower you’re hoping starts next spring. That’s normal. Battery tools have quietly taken over the homeowner workshop — they’re better than the gas and corded stuff they replaced in almost every way — but they brought a new set of skills with them, and nobody handed out the manual. How you charge, store and winter a lithium-ion pack now matters more than how you oil a hinge ever did.

This hub is that manual. It’s organized the way problems actually show up: something won’t work, something needs charging or storing, you’re buying into a platform, or you’re setting up the space itself. Each section points to a full guide.

When something won’t work

Start here, because this is why most people land on a page like this. Nine times out of ten the “broken” thing isn’t broken.

If a tool runs rough, stops under load, or won’t start at all, work through the cordless tool troubleshooting guide — it separates the two-minute fixes (contacts, battery seating, overheat lockouts) from the genuine failures, and it’s honest about which repairs a homeowner should not attempt.

If a battery won’t charge, the charger’s blinking light is trying to tell you something specific. Most “dead” packs are actually in a temperature lockout or a protective deep-sleep, not dead. The battery won’t charge troubleshooting guide decodes the light patterns brand by brand, and the safe battery revival guide covers what you can legitimately do about a pack the charger refuses — and why the jump-start trick all over YouTube is the one method to skip.

Batteries: the part that actually needs you

Here’s the mental shift battery tools require: the tool is durable, the battery is perishable, and almost everything that shortens a battery’s life happens while you’re not using it. Left on the charger for weeks. Stored dead. Stored full. Frozen in the garage all winter. Baked on a sunny windowsill in July.

What kills lithium-ion tool batteries (roughly in order)
Stored dead (deep discharge)
Worst
Heat - hot garage, sun, hot charging
Severe
Living on the charger at 100%
High
Charging below freezing
High
Normal use and honest wear
Low

The counter-moves are simple and they’re spelled out in three guides. How to charge cordless tool batteries safely covers the rules that matter: room-temperature charging (roughly 40–104°F), original chargers only, off the charger when done. How to store cordless power tools safely makes the case for the battery-first approach — packs out of the tools, at 40–50% charge, somewhere that doesn’t freeze or bake. And the battery tool safety guide covers the part nobody wants to think about: what thermal runaway is, how to spot a failing pack (swelling, heat, odor), and where damaged batteries actually go — hint: never the trash, and not the retail drop-off bin either.

If you’re ready to give charging a proper home instead of a random outlet, the charging station setup guide covers the wall, and the fire-resistant battery cabinet guide covers containment for households with a lot of packs.

Keeping tools alive

Maintenance on cordless tools is refreshingly small — no oil, no spark plugs, no carburetors — but it’s not zero, and the few tasks that exist are the difference between a drill that lasts fifteen years and one that cooks its motor in three. The cordless tool maintenance guide covers the whole loop: vents, contacts, chucks, and the safe wipe-down methods that don’t push liquid into a motor. The air compressor guide handles the one shop machine that can rust itself to death from the inside if you never drain the tank.

Outdoor power gear gets its own pair: how to maintain battery lawn tools for the in-season care, and how to store a battery mower, trimmer and blower for winter for the October ritual that decides whether spring starts with mowing or with troubleshooting.

Buying in: platforms before tools

The single most consequential decision in cordless tools isn’t which drill to buy — it’s which battery platform to marry. Batteries and chargers only work within one brand’s system, they’re where the real money goes, and switching later means abandoning that investment. Before any individual tool purchase, read how to choose the right battery platform; if you’re weighing the big shop brands head-to-head, the DeWalt vs Milwaukee vs Ryobi vs Makita ecosystem comparison lays out what each system is genuinely good at without pretending there’s one right answer.

Then, and only then, decide what to actually buy. Cordless power tools homeowners actually need is the anti-shopping-list: the handful of tools that earn their place, the ones that can wait, and the ones most people regret. On the yard side, battery mower vs gas mower settles the biggest single question with real trade-offs instead of cheerleading.

The space itself

A workshop that fights you gets abandoned, and most homeowner workshops fail on the same three things: not enough light where the cutting happens, no dedicated home for charging so batteries end up on whatever surface was empty, and a floor plan that puts the person between the tool and the door. How to use power tools safely in a small garage covers the layout, lighting and workflow habits that make a one-car garage work like a shop — where the cutting happens, where the batteries live, what needs light, and the handful of habits that prevent the classic small-space accidents.

Quick answers

What charge level should batteries be stored at? Around 40–50%, off the charger, somewhere that stays above freezing. Full and empty are both bad places for a lithium-ion pack to sit.

Why won’t my battery charge after winter in the garage? Usually cold lockout (warm it indoors a couple of hours) or deep discharge from sitting — the charger reads a deeply drained pack as defective. The won’t-charge guide walks the whole diagnosis.

Are battery tools worth it over gas and corded? For nearly all homeowner use, yes — instant start, no fuel, little maintenance. The honest exceptions are all-day heavy work and a few big-acreage yard cases, covered in the mower comparison.

Do I really have to stay on one battery brand? Within a system, yes — packs aren’t cross-compatible, and adapter workarounds void warranties. That’s why the platform decision comes before the first tool.

Where do old tool batteries go? Free drop-off bins at major hardware retailers handle healthy packs (tape the terminals). Swollen or damaged packs are different — those go through household hazardous waste. Details in the safety guide.

The bottom line

Battery tools rewrote the homeowner workshop, and the skills that matter now are battery skills: charge at room temperature, store at half-charge, keep packs out of the heat and cold, decode the blinking light before declaring anything dead, and treat a swollen pack as the hazard it is. What a Smart Panel Is and How It Actually Works
A plain-English tour of the smart electrical panel: what is inside the box, how per-circuit metering and relays work, and what the app layer really controls.

A smart panel is your home's electrical panel — the breaker box — rebuilt with three additions a conventional panel never had: a current sensor watching every circuit, a controllable switch behind every breaker, and a small computer connecting both to an app. That's the entire concept. The breakers still do their hundred-year-old safety job of tripping on overloads; the smart layer rides on top, measuring and steering the power the breakers protect. This guide opens the box: what's physically inside, how each layer works, and what actually happens when you tap a circuit off in the app. ## The box, layer by layer
Inside a smart panel: three layers on one backbone
Layer 1 — Protection (unchanged)Breakers trip on overcurrent, mechanically and locally, exactly like a conventional panel. Code-required, works with no network, no app, no power to the brain.
Layer 2 — SensingA current transformer (CT) on every circuit reads its draw continuously. Paired with voltage, that's live power (kW) and accumulated energy (kWh), per circuit.
Layer 3 — ControlA relay in line with each circuit can open or close it on command — from the app, a schedule, or an automation rule.
The brainA controller ties it together: collects readings, runs the rules, talks to your network and the vendor's cloud.
The layering explains the failure behavior people worry about. If the internet drops, Layer 1 doesn't care — protection is mechanical and local. You lose the remote dashboard until connectivity returns, and depending on the product, locally-running schedules may continue. If the brain itself failed, breakers still protect; you'd lose the smarts until service. The safety layer was deliberately kept boring. ## What the sensing layer gives you Per-circuit metering sounds incremental and is actually the transformation: it converts "my bill went up" into "the water heater circuit used 380 kWh." Every circuit reports continuously, so the app can rank your loads, show the always-on baseline, and graph any circuit's day. It's the same visibility a standalone [energy monitor](/smart-panel-vs-home-energy-monitor/) provides — built in, on every circuit, with no CT wiring project. If visibility is *all* you want, that cheaper monitor is the honest buy; the panel's case rests on the next layer. ## What the control layer adds Relays turn readings into actions. The manual version is app control — the garage heater goes off from your phone. The scheduled version handles recurring logic — the EV charger only enabled during off-peak hours. The automated version is where smart panels earn their keep: **load management**, where the brain watches total household draw and throttles or pauses designated big loads so the sum never exceeds what your service can carry. That's the mechanism that can fit an EV charger onto a service that would otherwise need a [costly upgrade](/smart-panel-vs-main-panel-upgrade/), and it's covered in depth in [smart panel vs load management device](/smart-panel-vs-load-management/). The same machinery powers **backup prioritization**: on battery power during an outage, the panel sheds circuits in your chosen order so the reserve stretches — the dynamic trick a fixed [transfer switch](/smart-panel-vs-transfer-switch/) can't do. ## What it looks like in the app Day to day, the app is four screens. A **live view**: every circuit's draw right now, the whole-home total, and (with solar) production versus consumption. A **history view**: kWh per circuit by day, week, month — the bill, itemized. A **controls view**: toggles and schedules per circuit. And a **rules view**: the load-management priorities and backup rankings you set once and mostly forget. The daily experience is honestly mundane — glance at the live view, notice the anomaly, adjust a schedule seasonally. The drama is reserved for the moments the automation quietly prevents: the overload that didn't happen, the battery that lasted the night. ## What a smart panel is not Three boundary lines keep expectations honest. It is not a [smart meter](/smart-panel-vs-smart-meter/) — that's the utility's billing device outside; the panel is your management device inside. It is not a [smart home hub](/smart-panel-vs-smart-home-system/) — it switches circuits, not bulbs, and can't run a lighting scene. And it is not extra capacity — load management stretches the amps you have by timing loads apart, but if your true simultaneous demand exceeds the service, only a [real upgrade](/smart-panel-vs-main-panel-upgrade/) adds amps. Knowing the borders tells you which product each job belongs to. ## Where the design trade-offs live Everything above describes the category; products differ in architecture. Some (like SPAN) build sensing and control into every position of a full replacement panel; others (like Leviton's approach) put the intelligence into individual smart breakers you add selectively; others (like Lumin) bolt a smart companion beside your existing panel — the full landscape is mapped in our [smart panels compared](/best-smart-panels-2026-guide/) overview and the [whole-box-or-one-at-a-time](/smart-panel-vs-smart-breakers/) comparison. The layers are the same in all of them; what varies is how many circuits get Layer 2 and 3, and what box they arrive in. ## A day in the life of the brain The clearest way to feel how the layers cooperate is to follow one ordinary evening. At 5:40 p.m. the oven preheats and the dryer starts — two heavy draws the sensing layer reports instantly. Total household demand climbs toward the ceiling you configured, so the brain consults its priority list and quietly signals the EV charger — lowest priority, most flexible — to pause. Layer 3's relay doesn't trip anything; the charger simply idles. Dinner proceeds; nobody notices. At 9:15 p.m. the dryer finishes and demand falls. The brain, still watching Layer 2's numbers, releases the charger, which ramps back to full current in time to exploit the off-peak window that begins at ten — a schedule you set once in the rules view. Overnight, the car fills at the cheapest rate while the house idles at its always-on floor, a number you've been slowly shrinking since the panel's history view first exposed it. At 2 a.m., a storm takes the grid down. The battery's gateway islands the home; the brain switches to its backup priority list and sheds the water heater, the EV charger, and the guest-suite circuits within seconds. The refrigerator, furnace fan, and network stay lit. As the battery drains past 40% near dawn, the second shedding tier drops two more circuits — stretching the reserve until the sun and the rooftop panels arrive. Every step was Layer 2 informing the brain, the brain commanding Layer 3, and Layer 1 standing guard the entire time, unchanged since your grandparents' breaker box. ## Quick answers **Does a smart panel still protect like a normal panel?** Yes — identical code-required breakers handle overcurrent, mechanically, regardless of network or app. **What happens when the internet is down?** Protection continues untouched; remote features pause. Local schedules may continue depending on the product. **Does it save energy by itself?** No hardware does — it creates the visibility and automation through which savings happen: rankings, schedules, load shifting. **Is it the same as a smart meter?** No — the meter is the utility's whole-home billing device; the panel is your per-circuit management device. [Full comparison here](/smart-panel-vs-smart-meter/). ## The bottom line A smart panel is a conventional breaker box with sensing, control, and a brain layered over unchanged protection: every circuit measured, every circuit switchable, automation stitching the two into load management and backup prioritization. Understand the three layers and every product in the category — and every comparison on this site — snaps into place. Start with whether you need Layers 2 and 3 at all; the [do-you-need-one decision guide](/do-you-need-a-smart-panel-a-clear-no-nonsense-homeowner-decision-guide/) walks that call. *Educational content, not professional electrical advice. Panel work must be performed by a licensed electrician to local code. Explore what per-circuit control could do for your loads in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

Why Your Battery Wont Charge: Troubleshooting Lawn Tool Packs
Mower or trimmer battery not charging? Decode the flashing charger light, fix cold and deep-sleep lockouts, and learn when a lithium pack is truly done.

It's the first warm Saturday of spring. You pull the mower battery off the garage shelf where it spent the winter, drop it on the charger, and get... a blinking red light. The manual, if you can find it, says that means "defective." Here's the thing the blinking light doesn't tell you: a huge share of lawn tool batteries flagged as defective every spring are nothing of the sort. They're cold, or they're deeply discharged into a protective sleep, or their contacts are dirty — all recoverable, all common, and all diagnosable in your kitchen in under an hour. Let's read what the charger is actually saying. ## What the blinking light means (it's not one thing) Chargers talk in light patterns, and the patterns mean different things — sometimes opposite things — across brands. Broadly, there are three messages: **"Charging normally."** On several platforms a flashing light IS the charging indicator — on many DeWalt chargers, for example, a blinking red light means the pack is charging and turns solid when full. Plenty of "broken" batteries are just batteries whose owner expected solid and saw blinking. Check your charger's label before anything else. **"Temperature hold."** A distinct pattern — red-green alternating on many Makita chargers, a long-flash/short-flash on some Black+Decker units, all-LEDs-flashing on others — means the pack is too hot or too cold to charge safely, and the charger is waiting. Lithium-ion packs generally accept a charge only within roughly 40–104°F, and the charger enforces it. **"Fault."** A rapid or erratic flash — rapid red on Makita and several others, alternating red/green on Ryobi-style chargers — means the charger's electronics measured something out of spec inside the pack: a cell voltage too low, too high, or an open circuit. This is the "defective" verdict. Sometimes it's right. Often, with an over-wintered pack, it's a deeply discharged battery the charger refuses to touch — a recoverable condition we'll get to. ## The kitchen-table diagnostic Work this in order; each step is a couple of minutes. **1. Warm it up.** If the battery lived in a cold garage, bring it — and ideally the charger — indoors and wait two hours. Not on a heater, not in the oven, just room temperature. Cold is the single most common cause of spring charging refusals, and it fixes itself with patience. A pack below freezing shouldn't be charged at all; charging below about 32°F can permanently damage lithium cells even when it appears to work. **2. Clean and reseat.** Pull the pack, look at both sets of contacts. Grass dust, oxide film and cobwebs all add resistance. Dry cloth first; isopropyl alcohol on a swab for the stubborn film; fully dry before reconnecting. Then seat the pack gently and completely — one widely-shared discovery among frustrated owners is that slamming a pack onto a charger can bounce the contacts, while a slow, deliberate seat gets a green light. **3. Blame the charger, not just the battery.** Test the outlet with a lamp. If you have a second pack, try it on this charger; if you have a second charger, try this pack on it. Chargers fail more often than people assume — a charger that shows no light on any battery, on a known-live outlet, is your culprit, and replacing a charger is far cheaper than replacing packs one by one while the real problem laughs. **4. Check the fuel gauge.** Most packs have an onboard button and LED gauge. Press it. Any lights at all means the pack has usable voltage and the problem is more likely temperature, contacts or charger. No lights at all points to deep discharge — keep reading. ## The deep-sleep problem: why stored batteries "die" Lithium-ion packs self-discharge slowly all winter, and the battery management system (BMS) inside the pack sips a little power on top of that. Store a pack half-empty in October, and by April its cell voltage can drop below the floor the BMS considers safe. At that point the BMS locks the pack down — a protective deep sleep — and here's the trap: the charger sees a voltage so low it reads as a damaged cell, flashes its fault pattern, and refuses to charge. The pack isn't dead. The pack is protecting itself, and the charger is being cautious. Two legitimate recovery moves exist. First, the pulse method: put the pack on the charger for a few seconds, remove it, repeat several times. Short exposure often nudges enough charge in to lift the pack above the lockout threshold, at which point a normal charge starts. Second, some smart chargers have a low-voltage recovery mode that trickles automatically — leave the pack on for an hour and check back. If neither works, there is a third method circulating online involving jumping the pack from a healthy battery — and that one crosses a safety line. The full picture, including exactly why, lives in the [safe lithium battery revival guide](/how-to-revive-a-dead-lithium-battery-safe-methods-only/).
i
The prevention is embarrassingly easy: store packs at 40–50% charge, indoors, and top them back to half-charge once mid-winter. A pack stored that way in October charges without drama in April. The full winter routine is in the lawn tool winter storage guide.
## When the battery really is done

Worth recovering

  • Fault light after winter storage, gauge shows nothing
  • Charges after warming to room temperature
  • Fuel gauge lights up but charger is dead on all packs
  • Charges fine but was simply forgotten half-seated
VS

Done - replace and recycle

  • Swollen, cracked, hot to the touch, or smells sweet/chemical
  • Fault code persists on two chargers at room temperature
  • Charges to "full" but dies in minutes under load
  • Several years old with steadily shrinking runtime
Two of those deserve emphasis. A pack that charges to full but collapses the moment the mower blade engages has high internal resistance from worn cells — voltage without current, the battery equivalent of an engine that idles but can't drive. No recovery method fixes worn-out chemistry; lawn tool packs typically give roughly three to five seasons depending on care. And a swollen or damaged pack isn't a candidate for anything except careful disposal: don't charge it, don't trash it, don't drop it in the retail recycling bin (those bins are for healthy batteries — tape the terminals first). Damaged packs go through your household hazardous waste program. The [battery safety guide](/battery-tool-safety-for-homeowners-essential-guide/) covers handling and disposal properly. One more modern wrinkle: warranty. Lawn tool batteries frequently carry multi-year coverage — some outdoor platforms warranty batteries for three to five years — and a genuine cell fault inside that window is a free replacement. People jump-start hundred-dollar packs that the manufacturer would have swapped for nothing. Check the paperwork before the pliers. ## Quick answers **Why is my battery flashing red and green on the charger?** On most platforms that's either a temperature hold (too hot or too cold — wait it out at room temperature) or a fault verdict on a deeply discharged pack. Warm it, clean the contacts, then try the pulse method before writing it off. **Can a completely dead lawn tool battery be saved?** Often, if "dead" means deep-discharged from storage: warm it up, pulse it on the charger, or use a charger with recovery mode. Not if the cells are worn out or the pack is physically damaged. **How long do battery mower batteries last?** Commonly around three to five years of seasons, heavily influenced by storage habits. Half-charged indoor winter storage is the single biggest life-extender. **Should I leave the battery on the charger all winter?** No. Charge to about half, take it off, store it indoors, and top it up once mid-winter. Living at 100% on a charger ages cells and, per fire-safety guidance, unattended charging is a risk that buys you nothing. **The battery works in the tool but won't charge — how?** A pack can have enough charge to run a light load while sitting below the charger's acceptance threshold, or one set of contacts is dirty while the other still connects. Clean everything, seat slowly, and test on a second charger. ## The bottom line A blinking charger is a message, not a death certificate. Warm the pack, clean the contacts, prove the charger, check the gauge — and if winter storage drained it into a protective sleep, pulse it back gently. Real death looks different: swelling, heat, persistent fault codes at room temperature, or a pack that quits under load. Those get recycled properly and replaced, ideally under the warranty you forgot the battery had. *Educational content, not professional advice. Never open, puncture or jump-start a battery pack, and dispose of damaged packs through hazardous-waste channels. Curious what charging your gear costs per season? Try the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/).*

It’s the first warm Saturday of spring. You pull the mower battery off the garage shelf where it spent the winter, drop it on the charger, and get… a blinking red light. The manual, if you can find it, says that means “defective.” Here’s the thing the blinking light doesn’t tell you: a huge share of lawn tool batteries flagged as defective every spring are nothing of the sort. They’re cold, or they’re deeply discharged into a protective sleep, or their contacts are dirty — all recoverable, all common, and all diagnosable in your kitchen in under an hour. Let’s read what the charger is actually saying.

What the blinking light means (it’s not one thing)

Chargers talk in light patterns, and the patterns mean different things — sometimes opposite things — across brands. Broadly, there are three messages:

“Charging normally.” On several platforms a flashing light IS the charging indicator — on many DeWalt chargers, for example, a blinking red light means the pack is charging and turns solid when full. Plenty of “broken” batteries are just batteries whose owner expected solid and saw blinking. Check your charger’s label before anything else.

“Temperature hold.” A distinct pattern — red-green alternating on many Makita chargers, a long-flash/short-flash on some Black+Decker units, all-LEDs-flashing on others — means the pack is too hot or too cold to charge safely, and the charger is waiting. Lithium-ion packs generally accept a charge only within roughly 40–104°F, and the charger enforces it.

“Fault.” A rapid or erratic flash — rapid red on Makita and several others, alternating red/green on Ryobi-style chargers — means the charger’s electronics measured something out of spec inside the pack: a cell voltage too low, too high, or an open circuit. This is the “defective” verdict. Sometimes it’s right. Often, with an over-wintered pack, it’s a deeply discharged battery the charger refuses to touch — a recoverable condition we’ll get to.

The kitchen-table diagnostic

Work this in order; each step is a couple of minutes.

1. Warm it up. If the battery lived in a cold garage, bring it — and ideally the charger — indoors and wait two hours. Not on a heater, not in the oven, just room temperature. Cold is the single most common cause of spring charging refusals, and it fixes itself with patience. A pack below freezing shouldn’t be charged at all; charging below about 32°F can permanently damage lithium cells even when it appears to work.

2. Clean and reseat. Pull the pack, look at both sets of contacts. Grass dust, oxide film and cobwebs all add resistance. Dry cloth first; isopropyl alcohol on a swab for the stubborn film; fully dry before reconnecting. Then seat the pack gently and completely — one widely-shared discovery among frustrated owners is that slamming a pack onto a charger can bounce the contacts, while a slow, deliberate seat gets a green light.

3. Blame the charger, not just the battery. Test the outlet with a lamp. If you have a second pack, try it on this charger; if you have a second charger, try this pack on it. Chargers fail more often than people assume — a charger that shows no light on any battery, on a known-live outlet, is your culprit, and replacing a charger is far cheaper than replacing packs one by one while the real problem laughs.

4. Check the fuel gauge. Most packs have an onboard button and LED gauge. Press it. Any lights at all means the pack has usable voltage and the problem is more likely temperature, contacts or charger. No lights at all points to deep discharge — keep reading.

The deep-sleep problem: why stored batteries “die”

Lithium-ion packs self-discharge slowly all winter, and the battery management system (BMS) inside the pack sips a little power on top of that. Store a pack half-empty in October, and by April its cell voltage can drop below the floor the BMS considers safe. At that point the BMS locks the pack down — a protective deep sleep — and here’s the trap: the charger sees a voltage so low it reads as a damaged cell, flashes its fault pattern, and refuses to charge. The pack isn’t dead. The pack is protecting itself, and the charger is being cautious.

Two legitimate recovery moves exist. First, the pulse method: put the pack on the charger for a few seconds, remove it, repeat several times. Short exposure often nudges enough charge in to lift the pack above the lockout threshold, at which point a normal charge starts. Second, some smart chargers have a low-voltage recovery mode that trickles automatically — leave the pack on for an hour and check back. If neither works, there is a third method circulating online involving jumping the pack from a healthy battery — and that one crosses a safety line. The full picture, including exactly why, lives in the safe lithium battery revival guide.

i
The prevention is embarrassingly easy: store packs at 40–50% charge, indoors, and top them back to half-charge once mid-winter. A pack stored that way in October charges without drama in April. The full winter routine is in the lawn tool winter storage guide.

When the battery really is done

Worth recovering

  • Fault light after winter storage, gauge shows nothing
  • Charges after warming to room temperature
  • Fuel gauge lights up but charger is dead on all packs
  • Charges fine but was simply forgotten half-seated
VS

Done - replace and recycle

  • Swollen, cracked, hot to the touch, or smells sweet/chemical
  • Fault code persists on two chargers at room temperature
  • Charges to "full" but dies in minutes under load
  • Several years old with steadily shrinking runtime

Two of those deserve emphasis. A pack that charges to full but collapses the moment the mower blade engages has high internal resistance from worn cells — voltage without current, the battery equivalent of an engine that idles but can’t drive. No recovery method fixes worn-out chemistry; lawn tool packs typically give roughly three to five seasons depending on care. And a swollen or damaged pack isn’t a candidate for anything except careful disposal: don’t charge it, don’t trash it, don’t drop it in the retail recycling bin (those bins are for healthy batteries — tape the terminals first). Damaged packs go through your household hazardous waste program. The battery safety guide covers handling and disposal properly.

One more modern wrinkle: warranty. Lawn tool batteries frequently carry multi-year coverage — some outdoor platforms warranty batteries for three to five years — and a genuine cell fault inside that window is a free replacement. People jump-start hundred-dollar packs that the manufacturer would have swapped for nothing. Check the paperwork before the pliers.

Quick answers

Why is my battery flashing red and green on the charger? On most platforms that’s either a temperature hold (too hot or too cold — wait it out at room temperature) or a fault verdict on a deeply discharged pack. Warm it, clean the contacts, then try the pulse method before writing it off.

Can a completely dead lawn tool battery be saved? Often, if “dead” means deep-discharged from storage: warm it up, pulse it on the charger, or use a charger with recovery mode. Not if the cells are worn out or the pack is physically damaged.

How long do battery mower batteries last? Commonly around three to five years of seasons, heavily influenced by storage habits. Half-charged indoor winter storage is the single biggest life-extender.

Should I leave the battery on the charger all winter? No. Charge to about half, take it off, store it indoors, and top it up once mid-winter. Living at 100% on a charger ages cells and, per fire-safety guidance, unattended charging is a risk that buys you nothing.

The battery works in the tool but won’t charge — how? A pack can have enough charge to run a light load while sitting below the charger’s acceptance threshold, or one set of contacts is dirty while the other still connects. Clean everything, seat slowly, and test on a second charger.

The bottom line

A blinking charger is a message, not a death certificate. Warm the pack, clean the contacts, prove the charger, check the gauge — and if winter storage drained it into a protective sleep, pulse it back gently. Real death looks different: swelling, heat, persistent fault codes at room temperature, or a pack that quits under load. Those get recycled properly and replaced, ideally under the warranty you forgot the battery had.

Educational content, not professional advice. Never open, puncture or jump-start a battery pack, and dispose of damaged packs through hazardous-waste channels. Curious what charging your gear costs per season? Try the Sim-Lab.

Why Your EV Charging Cost Changes Month to Month
Home EV charging cost is never identical each month. The real reasons behind the swing, from winter cold to mileage and rates, and how to smooth it.

If you track your EV charging cost month to month, you will notice it never sits still, and that variability is completely normal rather than a sign something is wrong. Your charging bill is the product of how many miles you drove, how efficiently the car used energy that month, and what your utility charged per kWh, and all three of those move. Understanding the swing turns a confusing bill into a predictable one, and it helps you budget from reality rather than from a single number. This guide breaks down each cause of month-to-month change and shows how to smooth the variation so your charging line stays steady. ## The three moving parts
What makes each month different
Miles drivenA month with a trip or extra errands uses more energy, plainly.
Efficiency that monthCold weather and short trips raise kWh per mile; mild months lower it.
Your rate that monthUtilities adjust rates seasonally, and TOP timing shifts your effective rate.
Every monthly difference is one of these three, or a combination. More miles obviously means more energy. Lower efficiency, mainly from winter cold, means more kWh for the same miles. And a higher rate, whether from a seasonal utility adjustment or from charging during peak hours, means more dollars per kWh. Because all three can move at once, and sometimes in the same direction, a cold, high-mileage winter month with peak charging can look dramatically more expensive than a mild summer month, even though nothing is broken. Our [cost-to-charge guide](/how-much-does-it-cost-to-charge-an-ev-at-home-2026-guide/) lays out the underlying formula these factors feed. ## Winter is the biggest swing The largest single cause of monthly variation for most drivers is the seasonal efficiency change, and winter dominates it. In cold months your car spends energy heating the cabin and battery and runs less efficiently, raising kWh per mile by anywhere from 10 to 30 percent, which our [winter-versus-summer guide](/how-much-it-costs-to-charge-an-ev-in-winter-vs-summer-2026-guide/) details. That alone can make January noticeably pricier than July for identical driving. Summer air conditioning adds a smaller bump. The result is a seasonal curve rather than a flat line, and recognizing that shape is the first step to not being surprised by it.
Mild spring/fallBaseline, lowest
High summer (AC)Small bump
Deep winter (heat)The real peak
Illustrative seasonal shape for the same monthly mileage.
## Rate changes you do not control Beyond your own habits, utilities themselves change rates over time, and this quietly shifts your monthly cost. Many utilities charge more during high-demand seasons, and rates have been rising broadly, up around 7 percent nationally over the past year. On a time-of-use plan, seasonal peak windows can also shift. None of this is within your control, but knowing it explains why a month with identical driving and weather can still cost a little more than the same month a year earlier. The fix is not to fight the rate but to charge in the cheapest window available, as our [time-of-use guide](/smart-charging-time-of-use-tou-2026-home-charging-optimization-guide/) describes. ## Mileage: the obvious variable It is easy to overlook the simplest cause: you drove more. A month with a road trip, a holiday visit, or a busier schedule uses more energy and costs more, full stop. This is worth separating from the others because it is not a rate or efficiency problem to solve; it is just more driving, and the per-mile cost stayed the same. When a bill jumps, checking your mileage first often explains it entirely, and if the trip involved public fast charging, that premium adds on top, as our [higher-than-expected guide](/why-your-ev-charging-cost-is-higher-than-expected-2026-guide/) notes. ## Smoothing the variation
Budget from your worst month. Track a full year, note your highest-cost month, and budget from that. You will come in under it most months, and a cold high-mileage month never catches you short.
You cannot eliminate the swing, but you can make it painless. Budget from your highest realistic month rather than an annual average, so winter never surprises you. Adopting a time-of-use plan both lowers and stabilizes cost, since overnight charging avoids volatile peak rates. And tracking your actual charging kWh each month, from a smart charger or the car app, gives you a real baseline to plan against. With a conservative budget and off-peak charging, the month-to-month variation becomes a minor ripple rather than a source of stress. ## When a swing signals a real problem Most month-to-month variation is normal, but a few patterns are worth investigating rather than accepting, and knowing the difference saves money. A sudden jump with no change in weather or mileage can mean your off-peak schedule stopped working and charging drifted into peak hours, which is worth checking in your car or charger app. A steady climb beyond what cold and mileage explain can reflect a rate increase your utility applied, which the bill itself will show. And an unexpected spike often traces to public fast-charging sessions on a trip that you forgot to account for. The way to tell normal variation from a real problem is to attribute each month's change to a cause: miles, weather, rate, or charging location. If every dollar of a swing maps to one of those, it is expected. If a chunk has no explanation, that is the signal to check your charging schedule and your latest rate, since a broken off-peak schedule is the most common hidden culprit behind an unexplained increase. ## Quick answers **Is monthly variation normal?** Yes, entirely. It reflects real changes in your miles, efficiency, and rate, not a billing error. **Why is winter so much higher?** Cold cuts efficiency and cabin heating spends energy, raising kWh per mile by 10 to 30 percent for the same driving. **Can I stop the swing?** Not fully, but off-peak charging stabilizes the rate side, and budgeting from your worst month absorbs the rest. **What should I check when a bill jumps?** Mileage first, then whether cold or peak-hour charging or a road-trip fast charge was involved. **Should I worry about a higher winter month?** No. A 10 to 30 percent winter bump for the same driving is expected. Worry only if a jump has no explanation in miles, weather, or rate. **Does an off-peak plan reduce the swing?** Yes. Stabilizing the rate side removes one of the three moving parts, leaving mainly weather and mileage to vary. ## The bottom line Your EV charging cost changes month to month because three things move: how far you drove, how efficiently the car used energy in that month's weather, and what your utility charged. Winter cold is the biggest swing, mileage the most obvious, and seasonal rate changes the one you do not control. The variation is normal, and you tame it by charging off-peak to stabilize the rate and budgeting from your worst month so nothing surprises you. Once you can name the cause of any given month, the variation stops feeling like a mystery and becomes just another predictable rhythm of ownership, no more alarming than a higher heating bill in January or a bigger water bill in a dry summer. *Educational guidance based on mid-2026 US averages and typical seasonal effects; not a quote for your situation. Rates, climate, and driving vary; use your own data. Estimate your cost in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/).*

Why Your EV Charging Costs More or Less Than You Expected
When home charging costs surprise you, a short list of causes explains it: charging losses, fast charging, winter, or TOU peaks. How to diagnose yours.

Plenty of EV owners look at their first few electric bills and feel the charging cost is off, either higher than the tidy figure they read before buying, or, pleasantly, lower. Both surprises usually trace to the same short list of causes, and once you know them your number stops being a mystery. The estimates online use national-average rates and ideal conditions, while your bill reflects your rate, your climate, and how you actually charge. This guide walks the common reasons a cost lands above or below expectations, so you can diagnose your own and decide whether anything is worth changing. ## Why it might be higher
Public fast chargingDC fast stations cost 2-3x home rates. A few road-trip sessions inflate the average fast.
Peak-hour chargingOn a TOU plan, charging during expensive afternoon peaks costs far more per kWh.
Winter and short tripsCold cuts efficiency; more kWh per mile means a higher bill.
A high state rateNational-average estimates understate cost in pricey states.
The most common reason a bill runs high is that the estimate assumed the national-average rate near 18.8 cents, while your state charges more, sometimes twice as much. The next is charging pattern: if you are on a time-of-use plan but charging during the afternoon peak, you are paying premium rates instead of the cheap overnight window, which our [time-of-use guide](/smart-charging-time-of-use-tou-2026-home-charging-optimization-guide/) explains how to fix. Occasional public DC fast charging, at two to three times the home rate, quietly pulls up your average. And winter cold raises kWh per mile, as our [seasonal guide](/how-much-it-costs-to-charge-an-ev-in-winter-vs-summer-2026-guide/) covers. ## Why it might be lower Just as often, owners are surprised the cost is lower than expected, and that is worth understanding too so you can lean into it. The biggest reason is off-peak charging: if your utility has a time-of-use plan and you charge overnight, you may be paying half the national-average rate, making your cost per mile remarkably small. Solar owners who charge from their own panels effectively pay a fraction of grid rates. Efficient sedans use fewer kWh per mile than the truck-heavy averages some estimates assume. And light drivers simply use less energy. If your bill is lower than the headlines, you are likely doing several of these things right, and the lesson is to keep doing them.
A low bill is a signal, not luck. Off-peak charging, solar, an efficient car, and modest miles each push your cost below the averages. If yours is low, you have found the levers worth protecting.
## The hidden ten percent: charging losses One genuine gap between expectation and bill catches nearly everyone: charging losses. Your car reports how many kWh it added to the battery, but your utility bills the kWh drawn from the wall, which is about ten percent higher on a Level 2 charger because some energy becomes heat. So if you calculated cost from the car's added-kWh figure, your real bill will run roughly ten percent above your estimate, and that is expected, not an error. Our [power-use guide](/how-much-does-a-level-2-charger-add-to-your-electric-bill-2026-guide/) explains why wall energy always exceeds battery energy. ## Diagnosing your real number
A quick diagnostic path
1. Find your true rateRead the price per kWh on your bill, including any delivery charges, not a national average.
2. Check when you chargeOn a TOU plan, confirm charging happens in the off-peak window, not the peak.
3. Separate fast chargingPull public DC fast sessions out; they belong to road trips, not daily cost.
4. Recompute with lossesUse wall kWh, or add ~10% to the car's figure, then multiply by your rate.
Working through these four steps almost always resolves the surprise. Most often the culprit is a higher state rate than the estimate used, or charging at the wrong time on a TOU plan, both of which our other cost guides address. Once you have recomputed with your real rate, your actual charging times, fast charging set aside, and charging losses included, the number will make sense, and you will know exactly which lever to pull if you want it lower. ## When to actually change something Not every high number needs fixing. If you are already charging off-peak in a high-rate state, your cost is simply what electricity costs where you live, and it still likely beats gas. But if the diagnosis reveals peak-hour charging, avoidable fast charging, or a missed time-of-use plan, those are real savings waiting to be captured. The point of diagnosing is to separate costs that are baked into your location from costs that are habits you can change, and to spend your effort only on the latter, as our [lower-your-cost guide](/how-to-lower-your-ev-charging-cost-2026-guide/) lays out. ## The estimate-versus-reality gap, in perspective It is worth stepping back to see why online estimates and real bills diverge so predictably, because the gap is structural rather than anyone's error. Published estimates almost always use the national-average rate, an efficient reference car, ideal mild-weather efficiency, and no charging losses, which together produce the lowest plausible number. Your real bill uses your actual rate, your actual car, your actual weather, and the real ten percent charging loss, which together push the number up. So a real bill running somewhat above a published estimate is not a sign of a problem; it is the difference between an idealized reference and lived conditions. The value in diagnosing is not to force your bill down to the estimate, which may be impossible in a high-rate state, but to confirm that the gap is explained by known, expected factors rather than a fault like a failed off-peak schedule or a metering issue. Once every dollar of the gap has a name, the surprise is gone even if the number itself does not change. ## Quick answers **Why is my charging cost higher than the estimates?** Usually a higher state rate than the estimate assumed, peak-hour charging on a TOU plan, occasional fast charging, or winter cold. **Why might it be lower?** Off-peak charging, solar, an efficient car, or modest mileage, each pushing you below the averages. **Why is my bill above my own calculation?** Likely charging losses: the wall draws about ten percent more than reaches the battery. Use wall kWh. **Should I try to fix a high number?** Only the parts that are habits, like peak charging or avoidable fast charging. A high state rate is just your local price. **Could a broken charging schedule be the cause?** Yes, and it is a common one. If an off-peak schedule silently fails, charging happens at peak rates, inflating the bill. Check your car or charger app. ## The bottom line An EV charging cost that surprises you, high or low, almost always comes down to a short list: your real state rate versus the average, whether you charge off-peak or on peak, occasional fast charging, winter cold, and the ten percent charging losses your car does not show. Diagnose with your true rate and your actual charging times, set fast charging aside, and the number will make sense. Then fix only the habits, and let the local price be what it is. *Educational guidance based on mid-2026 US averages; it is not a quote for your situation. Rates, climate, and habits vary; use your own bill and charger data. Estimate your cost in the [Sim-Lab](/en/sim-lab/charging-cost/).*

The biggest reason is off-peak charging: if your utility has a time-of-use plan and you charge overnight, you may be paying half the national-average rate, making your cost per mile remarkably small. Solar owners who charge from their own panels effectively pay a fraction of grid rates. Efficient sedans use fewer kWh per mile than the truck-heavy averages some estimates assume. And light drivers simply use less energy. If your bill is lower than the headlines, you are likely doing several of these things right, and the lesson is to keep doing them.

A low bill is a signal, not luck. Off-peak charging, solar, an efficient car, and modest miles each push your cost below the averages. If yours is low, you have found the levers worth protecting.

The hidden ten percent: charging losses

One genuine gap between expectation and bill catches nearly everyone: charging losses. Your car reports how many kWh it added to the battery, but your utility bills the kWh drawn from the wall, which is about ten percent higher on a Level 2 charger because some energy becomes heat. So if you calculated cost from the car’s added-kWh figure, your real bill will run roughly ten percent above your estimate, and that is expected, not an error. Our power-use guide explains why wall energy always exceeds battery energy.

Diagnosing your real number

A quick diagnostic path
1. Find your true rateRead the price per kWh on your bill, including any delivery charges, not a national average.
2. Check when you chargeOn a TOU plan, confirm charging happens in the off-peak window, not the peak.
3. Separate fast chargingPull public DC fast sessions out; they belong to road trips, not daily cost.
4. Recompute with lossesUse wall kWh, or add ~10% to the car's figure, then multiply by your rate.

Working through these four steps almost always resolves the surprise. Most often the culprit is a higher state rate than the estimate used, or charging at the wrong time on a TOU plan, both of which our other cost guides address. Once you have recomputed with your real rate, your actual charging times, fast charging set aside, and charging losses included, the number will make sense, and you will know exactly which lever to pull if you want it lower.

When to actually change something

Not every high number needs fixing. If you are already charging off-peak in a high-rate state, your cost is simply what electricity costs where you live, and it still likely beats gas. But if the diagnosis reveals peak-hour charging, avoidable fast charging, or a missed time-of-use plan, those are real savings waiting to be captured. The point of diagnosing is to separate costs that are baked into your location from costs that are habits you can change, and to spend your effort only on the latter, as our lower-your-cost guide lays out.

The estimate-versus-reality gap, in perspective

It is worth stepping back to see why online estimates and real bills diverge so predictably, because the gap is structural rather than anyone’s error. Published estimates almost always use the national-average rate, an efficient reference car, ideal mild-weather efficiency, and no charging losses, which together produce the lowest plausible number. Your real bill uses your actual rate, your actual car, your actual weather, and the real ten percent charging loss, which together push the number up. So a real bill running somewhat above a published estimate is not a sign of a problem; it is the difference between an idealized reference and lived conditions. The value in diagnosing is not to force your bill down to the estimate, which may be impossible in a high-rate state, but to confirm that the gap is explained by known, expected factors rather than a fault like a failed off-peak schedule or a metering issue. Once every dollar of the gap has a name, the surprise is gone even if the number itself does not change.

Quick answers

Why is my charging cost higher than the estimates? Usually a higher state rate than the estimate assumed, peak-hour charging on a TOU plan, occasional fast charging, or winter cold.

Why might it be lower? Off-peak charging, solar, an efficient car, or modest mileage, each pushing you below the averages.

Why is my bill above my own calculation? Likely charging losses: the wall draws about ten percent more than reaches the battery. Use wall kWh.

Should I try to fix a high number? Only the parts that are habits, like peak charging or avoidable fast charging. A high state rate is just your local price.

Could a broken charging schedule be the cause? Yes, and it is a common one. If an off-peak schedule silently fails, charging happens at peak rates, inflating the bill. Check your car or charger app.

The bottom line

An EV charging cost that surprises you, high or low, almost always comes down to a short list: your real state rate versus the average, whether you charge off-peak or on peak, occasional fast charging, winter cold, and the ten percent charging losses your car does not show. Diagnose with your true rate and your actual charging times, set fast charging aside, and the number will make sense. Then fix only the habits, and let the local price be what it is.
